Vanta automates security and compliance for over 16,000 fast-moving companies like Ramp, Cursor, and Harvey... Invest Like The Best listeners get a special offer for $1,000 off at vanta.com/invest.

Ridgeline is the first end-to-end system of record with embedded AI for investment management firms... You can request a demo at ridgeline.ai.

十一、融资：从拒绝到 1 亿美元 A 轮

主持人： So one idea for one of these stories is around the ability to raise capital to fund the thing. I think when you started it, you knew you'd need capital, but you did not know you'd need the quantum of capital that you've ultimately raised and are spending to build the solution. And you hadn't raised money before. There were moments where it was really, really difficult — because I was there, I saw it. There were moments where it was extremely difficult to raise the money that you did, without which the company would not exist.

即将耗尽现金的死亡时刻

Rob： We've had some intense moments. It reminds me of probably early 2024 before we raised our Series A. We were at this point where we had done enough of the architecture, done enough of the design that we knew the chip architecture was sound. We had to go build it. There was a lot more to do.

We were ready to go into what's called the physical design stage. We needed to sign an agreement with a physical design vendor, which will cost you at least $40-$50 million. And then we had this realization, as the models were getting bigger and bigger, that we were going to need to build the entire cluster — not just the chip. We were going to need to build boards, interconnects, cold plates, figure out all the networking and everything. This was going to cost a lot more than the $15 million we had in the bank.

主持人： And you're like, man, that was scary.

Gavin： Yeah. You're sitting in that moment, you think, holy crap, we can't afford this. And I began looking at: huh, how hard is it to go back to Harvard?

30 页备忘录与全员被拒

Rob： At the end of '23, we put together this memo. We spent like a hundred hours on this because we're like — we have no idea how people are going to believe us when we ask for the amount of money we're about to ask for. It was 30 pages. It was extremely technical and in-depth, of all the different things we needed to build and all the milestones we needed to hit, and how the market was going to evolve, and all the new use cases, and the cost per token and all this modeling.

Then we went and talked to investors. And every major investor in the valley passed immediately. They were just like, "Okay, two kids that just finished Harvard, haven't taped out a chip, no test chip. Inference — like, who knows if this is going to be a big market? Everything's going to be training. The models still hallucinate. This could all be a bubble."

At the time, the biggest semiconductor fundraise for a Series A was around $40-$50 million. We were looking at this and we were tallying the bill. We think we're going to spend $100 million in the next 12 months. If we really want to do this — if we want to actually get to scale and get the performance we're talking about — this is going to be extremely capital intensive. How the hell are we going to pull this off?

拉面预算与债务桥贷

Gavin： One of our key ways we got started in this process — we thought to ourselves: what is the cheapest possible way we do this? We decided, well, if I made almost nothing, and if I ate nothing but ramen, then we could spend basically just the money for the mask of 11 tape and that would be that. We could probably do it on $30 million — an absurdly low number. And we actually went out and got a debt provider willing to lend us the money we needed to cross this barely-barely ramen-to-a-chip threshold. From there I think it was first to catalyze a series of, "Hey, maybe we can do one more thing, one more thing, one more thing."

雪球的开始

Rob： We're at this moment where we're like, if we really want to build this company — because we're not going to half-ass it. We're not going to do a test chip and spend years on it and let the entire AI market boom while we could be building the product. If we're going to do it, we're going to go all the way. We're going to need to find a way to get $100 million.

I remember Gavin and I were sitting down in the office in Cupertino late at night just looking at each other and we're like, "Could we cut 500k here? Could we cut 100K here? How long could we convince everyone not to take a salary?" And we're like, holy crap, the math is not going to close. We really need to solve this.

There was a period of a few weeks where you just go into survival mode and you call every person that could possibly know an investor and you're like: "We need $100 million to do this. If we do this, we think this could be one of the most important companies of all time. Do you know somebody that wants to take an aggressive bet, that wants to believe in us? Here's all the information. We're an open book. Here's the team. It's great people. We've been working super hard. We've done these things in record time. But we have these hundred things to go. Do you want to do this?"

The snowball starts and you get a million here, 2 million here, and you're like, "Okay, we're not going to run out of money this month." You get a $5 million check, $10 million check, and you're like, "Okay, maybe I can buy those FPGAs." The snowball happened where we were very lucky that we ended up putting it all together.

We had a board meeting, I showed everyone the spreadsheet, and we look at it and it's like 103 million. These are all soft commits. And we all look at each other and we say, "We're going to take it." That was the Series A. Luckily, it's been much easier since then, and we've raised almost a half dozen rounds since then — many of them from those investors just doubling and tripling down. That's allowed us to get to market so quickly. This rack would not be possible had we not been so aggressive.

TSMC 的信任

Gavin： I also think suppliers deserve a little bit of a commendation here. TSMC was willing to work with us back before we'd raised any of that hundred million dollars, back when it was still really, really scary. They actually let us get some of their emulators on extremely favorable terms, where we pay over many years — basically a big loan. It takes a lot of belief from your partners to do this. But at the end of that you come out with this very strong team, and all the folks who back you are not in it just out of pure financial incentive. They believe.

主持人： Why did TSMC believe, do you think?

Gavin： This is a great story. Even before you joined in, there was a SEMI event and I was one of the only young CEOs of semiconductors — I think it's kind of a novelty — they asked me to come in and speak. I get to the SEMI event and I am the only speaker there under 40, and the only person there under 30. I was at the time 22.

I go up and speak. There was a speaker dinner afterwards, and by pure luck I happened to sit next to this very senior TSMC VP. It's a very nice dinner. There's like the former CEO of ARM there. It's very bougie. Everyone's in a suit. And I'm there with this VP, and it turns out we both studied math in college. We get a little piece of paper and we begin talking in great detail about how modern AI models work at the actual per-tensor-by-tensor level. The guy just gets it. We begin talking about: how do you run this very effectively? Why is Louisville such a critical technology to make this work?

The following day, I get an email from TSMC saying, "Gavin, I want to work with Etched. Find a way to make it happen." They've been a great partner ever since.

十二、关于反共识投资

主持人： It's amazing to think about some of the tropes — and obviously, like, should break the fourth wall here. I'm a big Etched investor. I've been involved for a long time. I think the absolute world of you guys. So I'm incredibly biased in this conversation. I'm trying to ask questions that are broader and interesting and could be objections to what you're doing.

But it's so interesting to me that, like, when you read about investing, everyone cites this idea of "contrarian and right" as the quadrant that makes all the money. And it sounds really nice. But contrarian means everyone else thinks you're stupid. And so when you go and you get immediate nos from literally everybody, it is a fascinating quadrant to exist in before you become consensus.

Rob： What was it like for you? I'm super curious.

主持人： Well, it's interesting. At the time, it was the largest by a lot first check that I had written. Suffice to say, I'm not a math expert or a semiconductor expert or an AI expert really at the time. It was much more of: I believed in the concept of this market potentially being huge, you having made very, very clear bets on how the future was going to look, having positioned the company to attack those things in a hardcore way. And then just the two of you. And what I felt about you was the majority of the reason why we made the bet when we did in 2023 or whatever it was.

At the time it was the biggest. And I think the same thing you said about naivety applies to investing as does to maybe building a semiconductor startup — which is, I didn't know what I didn't know. When I called experts, they were basically like, "This is stupid." They laid out in very logical terms why this wasn't going to work and why it was such a low-probability bet.

One of the things I've learned from it is you kind of have to damn the base rate. If you invested on base rates, you should do something other than what we and I do. There's always the index fund.

Rob： Yeah, there's always an index fund.

主持人： Exactly. So it's actually never been scary for me. I think probably most of that is because — I don't know, there's a lot I don't know. And if I knew more about what you guys have done and the difficulty, I probably wouldn't have done it. I don't know what that says about maturing as an investor. Maybe I don't want to know a lot more and have some of that healthy naivety.

Rob： A lot of the traditional semiconductor funds missed the entire AI chip — all the AI chip companies. And all the coding experts missed all the coding companies. I think it's very hard to realize that constraints have changed. When you've looked at tape-outs for 20 years and you've seen so many of them not work on the first try or the second try or the third try, like you couldn't even run a workload — you totally forget that EDA tools are way better and that FPGAs exist today in a way that they didn't exist before, and all the types of validation you can do today just wasn't possible.

For us, a lot of our believers were on two sides of it: they were just believers in the market and the team, or they were building chips today and were extremely technical — like the high-frequency trading firms, where they would literally audit everything from the microarchitecture and the RTL to the board designs and the schedule and the software stack. We would sit down with 10 of their people who build their own chips, and they're asking us such detailed questions that we're wondering, "Are they going to build the chip?" It was really one of those sides. If you were anywhere in the middle, you just wouldn't understand it.

主持人： In the investing world, they often talk about variant perception — something that you see or believe that others don't, right? And that perception creates the opportunity. I think I've invested, I don't know, five or so times in that. And every time when you do it, stakes are getting bigger and bigger. So it does get a little scarier and scarier. And because you guys have been so quiet in the marketplace, I think it's very easy to dismiss you. As the stakes get bigger and bigger, those dismissals are harder to hear. So I do think like betting on something that you see when what you hear from the outside world is very different — that perception gap is opposite.

The last thing I would say is the accumulated evidence of your guys' and your team's ability to solve seemingly impossible problems is one of the most interesting things a company can have. It's like a binary — like companies do this or they don't.

Rob： That's the thing — a big advantage of people who have been here for a long time. You get some new joiners who are scared shitless when you see a thing like this, and there are old-timers who've been here for all of two years — smoking cigars in the trenches. "Another one. Another one."

Gavin： There's definitely a find-a-way mentality. If you're here, you're here because you assume it's possible. So we can't be saying it's impossible. Everything is solvable and we're just going to work at it until we figure it out.

Wafer Sort 那一夜

There's a favorite story I have, this guy who's kind of a legend in silicon validation who joined our team. We were doing the early stages of what's called wafer sort. When your chips are coming out of fab, they go out on these wafers, and you have this thing called a probe card that attaches to the wafer before you dice it with these probe pads, and you send these electrical signals to basically test which chips are good and bad. So when I slice the wafer into a bunch of chips, I can package only the good ones.

We go through our first wafer, and it's like 2-3:00 a.m. because we're doing it with TSMC over the phone in Taiwan. We have the screen with the wafer that's all gray, and each chip is gray. As you start running the patterns, the squares are supposed to turn green or red — and they all turn red.

We're like, this is really bad. Everybody's like, "Guys, take a breath." He leans back and he's like, "The puzzle begins."

You have to have the attitude of: yes, you will go out and you will stare into the abyss and you will see scary things, and we'll solve them.

主持人： When did you see the first green square?

Rob： Within a day of that. But in the moment you're like, "I have worked for years for this. I put my life on the line. I've asked my family to stake everything on it. And then it's red." It's extremely scary. There's a certain type of person who is addicted to that feeling — of feeling the fear and solving it. We are lucky to have a lot of those people here.

十三、面向下一代的思考

主持人： If you think about applying all of this earned know-how from these last several years and now thinking ahead to Gen 2, Gen 3 and beyond — what will you be doing most differently as a result of everything that you've learned?

真正决定推理胜负的三件事

Rob： It took us a while to get to the primitives that we think are really what matters for scaling inference. We tried a bunch of things early on — from compilers that would turn different models into FPGAs, to burning weights in silicon, to splitting your HBM to KV cache and weights, all these different things. There was a lot of cycles of learning till we got to the point that we realized fundamentally if you want to run a majority of the tokens in the world, you need to do three things:

  1. Build a chip with the most flops in a given power budget.
  2. Build a chip that has the lowest latency between other chips — so the biggest scale-up domain possible.
  3. Produce as much of it as possible.

In the first half of our journey so far, we learned the first two, and that informed the design a lot. That informs a lot about the bets we're making in the future with low voltage inference and cluster scale memory. But the production part, I think in the past year, has become extremely obvious. How much people want to deploy this stuff — if you can have it available today. The best ability is availability. If I have a thousand chips today, someone's going to use them. We need to build a chip that's not just way better than what's been built before, but it needs to be available at many-gigawatt scale. We need to build a product that is producible at gigawatts per month from the limit.

As we think about that, a lot of the design decisions we're making with our next gen, which you've seen already, is just about simplicity — removing tons of parts, trying to assemble and disassemble a thing again and again, learning how to make the cycle times as quick as possible in production, making sure it's going to be reliable, serviceable, and producible at gigantic scales.

供应链不是零和

主持人： What about other problems in the ecosystem that are outside of your control? Such as capacity at the leading nanometer at TSMC or availability of HBM4 memory, or some of these other things where everyone is fighting for scarce capacity. How do you face up against those realities?

Rob： The people deploying the most compute in the world do think about supply a bit zero-sum: there's only so many wafers being produced on a given nanometer node, only so much memory being produced. That's why actually for our first-gen product, we built it on a different supply chain than the Rubins. We're on 4-nanometer, Rubin's on 3-nanometer. We're on a different HBM than Rubin's, and so forth.

It actually is not a zero-sum thing — it's a positive sum where more is more. Often when we're talking to people deploying at scale, it's not a decision between a gigawatt of GPU and a gigawatt of us. It's 2 gigawatts. As much as possible, thinking about supply chain early in the design decisions — because if you have the most performant product and you can't produce it, then you're just a podcast.

Gavin： The other big thing about vertical integration too is: for certain things like the chips and the memory, you have to partner. Those are also very highly in-demand components. The more that you build yourself, the more stuff you can do on top of what the world can currently build. It is not "you're taking availability from somebody else." You're adding way, way more. That's how you win.

十四、模型本身的未来

主持人： One of the things I realized we haven't talked at all about is the models themselves, which is kind of crazy — the things behind all of this demand. Anything interesting that you would say about the way that you see models progressing? I guess I'm more interested in how you as thinkers about hardware think hardware might impact where the models themselves go in the future.

机器不像人那样思考

Gavin： One of the most important ideas that we believe in is machines don't think like people think. You look at airplanes, for example — airplanes don't fly like birds fly. When you think about how mechanical devices have to work, it's often very different.

In much the same way: for people, storing data and loading memory is very cheap for neurons, and doing math is relatively expensive. It is the exact opposite for chips. Generally, loading data is very expensive and doing math is very cheap. As time goes on, I think you will end up finding that math gets cheaper at a rate that is faster than memory gets cheaper, due to this fundamental limit on any kind of DRAM device.

You should think about: how can I make my model use a huge amount of compute? What if I had many copies running at the same time? What if I activated a huge number of experts? What if I had gigantic experts that I can run on multiple server racks at the same time? That is how I think you'll build models that are the next generation of intelligence.

And context too. There's been a lot of work on "very efficient inference — what if I don't load the full context into memory?" Most of the time I think that makes a lot of sense. You want to build a superintelligence — why can't it look at a billion tokens of context? Why can't it spend a huge amount of compute to read all that in super fast? I would love to be able to talk to a machine that was able to attend to, well, every book ever written, in its short-term memory. I think you're going to get to a point where you can.

动态性：模型架构与硬件的共生

Rob： A theme in models right now is this focus on something called dynamism — the ability to control the level of computation and memory spent at a per-token or per-user level when doing attention, as well as the ability to dynamically on the fly send data to other chips for different models doing certain types of operations.

The reason is, as we are scaling context length, scaling model size, scaling the amount of computation per user, we're looking for ways to be more efficient. The first thing — like Gavin says — mixture-of-experts architectures, where maybe we don't need every parameter being used for every token. Maybe there are things where, even at a token level, we can say, "Well, this token needs this context from this other token. They can share that memory, so we don't have to have the overhead of using the memory as much. Maybe this token is really important, so we should spend more compute. We should have longer context on that token."

Hardware that really accelerates these types of very dynamic computations is extremely important. You can imagine current hardware that was designed before those types of architectures had lots of overheads in doing them. So you basically end up in these really bad worlds where you have inefficient hardware at doing this dynamism — therefore you can't run it very well — or you have these very blocky architectures that are kind of applying blunt force to many different tokens that all need more or less computation.

十五、对未来的两个大问题

主持人： I have two questions about the future. The first is about the new ways that people might start using these systems — the raw technology, log of run times, things of this nature. When inference gets much cheaper, faster, more accessible — there's more total supply and it's better — what are the things that you think people will use that capacity to do that are the most interesting, exciting to both of you?

团队代理与代理总规模

Gavin： There was a viral tweet by Noam Brown where he said that as these models are having longer and longer time horizons, they can do tasks that take, say, six months. There's often not enough time to evaluate them for such a long period of time, because by that point you'll have a new model you'll want to evaluate instead. With tech like our cluster scale memory, you can run that six-month job much faster.

But there's a second piece of this too. Talking to him about it, he's now an angel as well, it's not just the time, it's also the number of people or agents working on it. If you're trying to evaluate "can a human build a rocket," you will find that the answer is no. No one person can build a rocket. Instead, you have to put a team together. And I believe the same thing will be true of agents too. If you want to ask "can agents build some crazy futuristic piece of software," you'll probably need a very large team. Maybe that's 10, maybe that's a million. You have to have this enormous amount of both cluster scale memory to have very short time per token, and a huge amount of flops to be able to run that whole fleet.

2027 年的预测

Rob： I'm going to be a little futuristic. I firmly believe we are on a global march of inference becoming a majority of global GDP. It may take more than 10 years, but it's going to happen. Right now we measure productivity as a society as GDP per capita, but really it's going to look much more like agents per megawatt — or maybe agents per gigawatt — by then.

And while we're being futuristic, I think this is the second to last year where a majority of the workforce is going to be human. I think in 2027, you're going to see more agents doing knowledge work than humans. It's going to be extremely interesting to see what happens.

You could imagine a world where, for countries, a majority of their energy ends up going into data centers doing inference, and the energy efficiency of those data centers basically governs how many agents — and therefore how big their workforce is. So you're going to see, as Gavin is saying, right now you have one agent or a team of 5 to 10 agents working on group projects for a couple days, and you can do pretty cool stuff because they're smart, but it's not going to be civilization scale. What happens when you have countries that can have literally a billion concurrent agents — like a billion people in the workforce working 24/7 concurrently on the same stuff? It's just kind of unfathomable what's going to happen. It's going to be the biggest proliferation of technology humanity's ever seen.

分布式大脑

Gavin： When you have these huge amounts of demand, you get this idea of economies of scale again. If you think about people, I have a brain. I'm not using the whole thing all at the same time — only a part of it is going to be active. This is the way healthy brains work. And for MoE models, it works much the same way. On an MoE model, only a small fraction of the parameters is being used for any given token at any given moment.

But if you have a large number of users on a piece of hardware, you can kind of take that brain, cut it up into many different experts on many different servers, and run a huge amount of volume through it. You'll have a bunch of different pieces of traffic. You'll have many of them using each part of the brain at any given point in time. And you'll also make the cost per thought, cost per token, way, way lower.

So I think you're going to end up with these giant distributed brains, which is going to look like — the real form factor of this is a big data center with a bunch of chips, a huge amount of flops, and a huge amount of scale-up interconnect.

主持人： You think we'll see a trillion-dollar individual data center?

Gavin： Absolutely. It is a matter of time. It's like asking, "Will you see a billion-dollar fab, or a 10 billion-dollar fab, or a 100 billion-dollar fab?" It is inevitable that the economies of scale don't stop at, "Oh, $40 billion is the magic number for fabs." No, the cost per wafer keeps going down as you keep spending more money. The same thing will be true of plants that make steel or plants that make tokens.

十六、对外星人讲机会

主持人： A very smart alien lands on Earth and wants to know from each of you how you would frame up this opportunity that you guys are tackling. What do you say to them?

Gavin： Frame it as: thinking is really valuable. Every company in the world runs on thinking. We are entering this really unique moment in time where you have machines that can think almost as good — and as soon as good and as soon better — than the best humans can. Building these machines is going to be a huge opportunity. But more important than that, the way in which you run this kind of thinking is going to be very different as demand goes higher and higher. There's a unique moment right now to build a new set of solutions, a new roadmap for how do you run the future quadrillion-parameter models for a billion people all at the same time on a gigantic scale-up cluster.

Rob： We are in a new era of intelligence where the cost of producing intelligence is dramatically so much cheaper than the value of the intelligence — that we are in a many-year, probably many-decade supply shortage of these tokens. Basically any chip or any system that can produce tokens is likely to be extremely valuable. You should find some part of the supply chain of the token — it can be everything from model training down to what we're doing in the silicon and otherwise — to spend time on and push the frontier.

The companies that are the largest are frankly going to be the companies that produce most of the global supply of tokens and own a majority of the supply chain of that token. And importantly, it's people who build systems that, as they get more and more chips put together, get cheaper. The way you want this to scale is not, "Oh, if I want to serve 10 times more tokens, I buy 10 times more servers." It must be some solution where I want to serve 10 times more tokens, then I get some economies-of-scale benefit with my cluster scale memory tech, which allows me to not charge as much as 10 times more for those tokens.

主持人： What a ridiculously exciting future that you guys are building to enable.

收尾问题：最善意的礼物

主持人： When I did this with Gavin last time, I asked him my traditional closing question. So this time I'll ask you, Rob — what is the kindest thing that anyone's ever done for you?

Rob： During my cancer treatment, there was a big decision I had to make. The doctors came to me and said, "It's time for you to decide. Do you want to get surgery or do you want to get radiation? Here's the trade-off. If you get surgery, you're more likely to live, but you have to assume you'll never be able to walk again. If you get radiation, you'll be able to walk again, but it's not the same probability that you'll live. You may die. What do you want to do?"

I was 16. My parents said, "You have to make this decision for yourself." I thought about it for a long time and decided I'm going to do the surgery. I get the surgery. One of the things you do when you get a tumor resection is they do something called a necrosis analysis, where they look at all the different cells and say: is a cell dead or alive? Because if you have a bunch of cancer cells alive, you have a problem. They looked at it and they said, "You usually want 98, 99% necrosis for us to say you're in the clear. You're below that. You should go get radiation."

There were only a few machines in the world that could do the type of radiation I needed. One of them was in Boston. I was in a wheelchair, and I needed to move to Boston for multiple months. And both of my parents decided to move out and drop everything they were doing and live with me.

I'm eternally grateful.

主持人： Beautiful. Thanks, guys. Amazing conversation.

【片尾广告】

Your finance team isn't losing money on big mistakes. It's leaking through a thousand tiny decisions nobody's watching. Ramp puts guardrails on spending before it happens... Try it at ramp.com/invest.

As your business grows, Vanta scales with you, automating compliance and giving you a single source of truth for security and risk. Learn more at vanta.com/invest.

Ridgeline is redefining asset management technology as a true partner, not just a software vendor... Visit ridgelineapps.com.

Every investment firm is unique, and generic AI doesn't understand your process. Rogo does... Check them out at rogo.ai/invest.

item.description:
## 一、最反直觉的洞察：他们卖的不是芯片，是「热力学套利」
大多数人以为 Etched 的优势是"为推理专门设计架构"——这是表层叙事。**真正的核心赌注是一个物理学套利：电压的平方与功耗成正比（Dennard 定律）**。GPU 必须以高电压运行，是因为它要兼容地球上所有可能的负载——从冰窖里的传感器到桑拿房里的手机。而 AI 数据中心永远不会低于 80°C。
这意味着 GPU 的高电压是在为**一种不存在的"通用性税"**买单。Etched 把电压砍掉一半以上，功耗骤降到四分之一以下——他们不是在"做得更好的芯片"，他们是在**回收行业为了"普适性幻觉"而支付了几十年的能量税**。
跨领域连接：这与**比特币矿机**的逻辑完全同构。Bitcoin ASIC 之所以能用 GPU 1/4 的电压运行，是因为它只做一件事。Gavin 在访谈里轻描淡写地提到这点，但这其实是整个公司的"原罪式洞察"——**专用性不是商业策略，是热力学策略**。
## 二、隐藏的系统性真相：他们在做"代币界的福特流水线"
Rob 反复提到"production is the product"——但

item.title: [a16z Deep Dives] How AI Is Redefining What It Means to Be Creative | Deep Dives with a16z
item.dc:creator: a16z Deep Dives
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、最反直觉的洞察：AI 不是在杀死摄影，而是在把"决定性瞬间"从快门时刻搬迁到后期

布列松那句神圣的"决定性瞬间"统治了摄影 80 年——它的本质是：你必须在快门按下的 1/125 秒里完成所有创造性决策，因为之后就什么都做不了了。Zach 在对话里轻描淡写地说出了一句颠覆性的话："摄影师在 post-capture 阶段比以前更有乐趣了。"

这句话的真正含义是：摄影的"创造时间窗口"正在从毫秒级被拉长到无限。这不是工具升级，而是介质的相变——就像电影从胶片走向数字时，剪辑从"物理切割"变成"非线性"，整个创作的时间结构被重写了。摄影师现在面对的不再是"瞬间艺术"，而是一种类似雕塑或绘画的"持续可塑介质"。这是一次悄无声息的范式革命，多数人还没意识到。

二、跨领域连接：AI 创作工具正在重演编程语言抽象层的百年历史——但跳过了中间步骤

听 Matt 谈"应该把抽象层画在哪条线上"，我立刻想到的是：从汇编 → C → Python → 自然语言编程，软件业花了 70 年才走到"用英语写程序"。而 AI 创作工具正在 3 年内压缩走完这条路，并且同时存在所有抽象层——Photoshop 的图层（汇编级）、节点工作流（C 级）、Canvas（高级语言）、纯文本 prompt（自然语言）共存于同一个用户的工作流里。

这里有一个被忽略的结构性后果：用户不再选"工具"，而是在选"抽象层"。这意味着创意工具公司未来真正的护城河不是模型，而是抽象层之间的可切换性（zoom in/zoom out）——就像 IDE 让你在调试时能从 Python 跳到 C 再跳到汇编。今天没有任何 AI 创作工具做到了这一点，这是下一个十亿美元产品的形状。

三、隐藏的系统性真相：所有受访者都在回避一个问题——模型应该会"问问题"

Man 提到了一句几乎一闪而过却最关键的话："如果模型能告诉你它需要什么输入，这是被严重低估的研究方向。"今天所有的 AI 工具都是单向街：用户说，模型做。但真正的创作合作从来不是单向的——画廊主问客户"你的客厅朝南还是朝北"，导演问演员"你觉得这个角色为什么留下来"。

更深一层的洞察是：当前的 prompt-engineering 文化本质上是把"沟通失败的责任"全部推给了用户。我们训练用户去揣摩模型需要什么，而不是训练模型去询问用户想要什么。这与心理治疗、教育学、产品访谈的所有人类经验完全相反——在那些领域，提问能力比回答能力更稀缺、更值钱。

预测：未来 18 个月内，第一个把"模型主动追问"做成核心交互的创意工具，会让所有 prompt 框看起来像 DOS 命令行一样陈旧。这不是 UI 改进，是权力关系的反转——从"驯化用户去喂模型"变成"模型谦卑地服务用户的意图"。这一反转的真正赢家将不是模型最强的公司，而是最懂临床访谈和苏格拉底式对话的公司。

彩蛋观察：好与差的"同源性"陷阱

Matt 最后那句"产出 slop 的模型，同一个模型也产出过惊艳的作品"——这句话的统计学含义比它听起来严肃得多。它意味着模型质量已经不是变量了，用户成了唯一变量。这与摄影史上的一个老定律惊人吻合：1888 年柯达推出"You press the button, we do the rest"之后，相机门槛被消除，但那一刻起，摄影才真正分化出"艺术家"与"按快门的人"。AI 创作工具正在制造同样的分化——而且差距会比柯达时代大得多，因为工具能力的上限被抬得更高了。

📝 转录文本

开场：创造力的本质与方向

"I think the creativity is building a story. The tools alone aren't a story. Someone has to direct them." "It's not about mastering those tools. It's about directing an agent who can use those tools to achieve your creativity."

主持人介绍： Today we have Zach Shia who is the co-founder and CTO of Photo Labs. We also have Man Tensic who is the head of applied research at Luma.

"Janet I have become so good. You can be sort of authentic to that moment while getting a little bit creative of stuff. So, I just think a lot of photographers are having more fun post capturing than before. How do you make something unique with the tools you have access to now? There has to be something more than just text. If you go to, say, a studio and you say, 'Make me a 10-second video about a dog jumping in the grass.' They're never going to take that deal. They're going to want more specific. And I think AI tools are no different."

"Something I heard from someone today earlier was they assume AI to be slop. It's up to the humans to create something out of it. What are your thoughts?" "I feel like that's such a hard question."

嘉宾介绍

Today we have Zach Sha who is the co-founder and CTO of Photo Labs. He works on personalized AR and AI photography. We also have Man Tensic who is the head of applied research at Luma. He works on agentic systems fundamental research and he was also the co-creator of Nerf. Thanks for coming on the pod.

一、艺术家与技术的角色：三年前 vs. 今天

Q： Matt, many years ago—maybe three years ago—you started this online talk about Nerf, and you posed a really interesting question: what is the role of the artist and what is the role of technology? Now fast forward three years, which is like a lifetime in AI, what is your answer then and what's your answer now? Has it changed?

Matt： Yeah, so if I recall correctly, my answer at that point was that really the point of this AI technology is to act as a tool for artists to better execute on the creative vision that they have. And at the time, Nerf was providing an ability for people to create 3D assets in a way that didn't require tons and tons of manual effort. Because when you're creating 3D scenes, creating those assets isn't really where the creative part is. It's how do you put them all together? How do you build a story around it? And that's where those tools were helpful.

I think now there's just so many more tools that just allow you to do that, not just in 3D, but also in video, in images. And I think this thesis that these AI technologies can be used as tools is even more so than it was in the past.

Q： And Zach, I know you worked at Adobe and then you did a lot of research that later went into Lightroom, which is also a creator tool. What's your view?

Zach： Yeah, I think artists really are where their creative minds are. They have a lot of creativity, and technology is sort of a way to help them express that kind of creativity. In the past few years, artists were also spending a lot of time trying to master the workflows, the tools. I don't think that's necessarily where their creativity is. Now their tools have become easier to use, more expressive than before. It's just so much easier for them to express the creativity—but I still think technology cannot replace the creative minds today.

二、什么是"创造力"？

Q： What would you define as creativity here? Because what models are creating—is that creativity? Or is it what the user composes together?

Zach： I think creativity is building a story. There are so many different ways to build a story, and you use tools to help construct that. The tools alone aren't a story. Someone has to direct them. I really think this directive mind is just so important. Everybody can use the tools, but the stuff that gets created from these tools is very different—and the difference of the things that are created, that's where the creativity is. People have different thoughts, different stories, or even just a simple thing they create which doesn't necessarily have a very strong story behind it, but they use the tool differently than others. I think that's where creativity is.

三、AI 时代的摄影：创造力时刻的迁移

Q： Matt is also a photographer and painter in his spare time. So what is AI creativity when it comes to photography? There's one school of thought that whatever you capture in the real world is the end result and you don't want to change pixels. The other view is that the camera is just one way to produce reality—painting was another, Nerf is another. How has your view changed?

Zach： Photography has always been a very creative process. A lot of the creativity in photography was more on the capturing side before this AI boom. People are trying to find the important moments, find the good landscape views, find the right angle, the right composition, use the right tools, wait for a very long time to take that photo. That's the creativity that photographers are really proud of.

Q： They wait and wait for that moment, right?

Zach： Exactly. The reason capturing was the most creative time is because the decisive moment—once it happened, there wasn't so much you could do to it afterwards. I feel like that part has changed a lot in the past three years. GenAI has become so good. You can be sort of authentic to the moment while getting a little bit creative. I just think a lot of photographers are having more fun post-capturing than before.

四、从研究者到产品建造者：学到了什么

Q： Both of you started your careers doing PhDs and very in-depth research. Since you went into industry, there's a lot of user-facing work—building the product, shipping it, learning from users. The research community cares more about benchmarking and optimization. How has the transition been? What did you have to learn, and where do you think the gap is?

Matt： As a researcher your goal is always to push the technology, so wherever there's a hole in the technology, you push it. That doesn't necessarily align with what a customer or end user would actually be interested in. So quite often you see these things diverge. For example, in image generation, an artist probably doesn't care about generating paragraphs of really dense text in some esoteric font—maybe some do, but most don't. But as a research question, that's extremely interesting. So you do have to decide: when do you take a step back and push the creative side? Because in many cases, what an artist wants is actually very simplistic, and the tools already exist—it's just a matter of piecing together in the right way.

Q： Like removing background itself is a boring task for most researchers I guess.

Matt： Yeah, but it's extremely useful for any creators out there. Or even just fixing up lighting of an image—it doesn't have a very concrete solution, so it's hard for a researcher to optimize a metric against, but when it comes to a creative task, it's extremely useful.

Zach： What I've learned, and am still learning, is that there's a fine balance. Researchers get excited about new technology, so they just want to push it forward. Users want you to solve the specific problem they just ran into. We can't just solve the day-to-day problem—we also want the technology to be a little bit ahead of users so we can reimagine their workflow. But at the same time, you want to make it balanced, so you're not producing a product no one is going to use.

With photography, at the beginning of this AI era we got a lot of pushback from photographers: "I just want to capture. I don't edit." Now they're slowly getting to "oh, it's fine to remove some distracting object in that image, it doesn't matter to me, and that's still my creativity." People are transitioning as well. Technology needs to stay a little bit ahead of that spectrum.

Q： That's such a great point. It is a chicken-and-egg problem. You can't implement 100% of what users ask. A seasoned Photoshop user may ask you to reinvent a lasso tool. The question is: do you want to do that? Is that the mental model you want your model to have? Maybe the next abstraction is there's no lasso tool—we're not implementing the world in the old Photoshop way.

五、用户超乎预期的用法

Q： When you work closely with creators, what is the surprising thing about their workflow? Anything that you pushed out there but wasn't designed for, that had a lot of adoption?

Matt： One of the first things that comes to mind is that you tend to think an artist or creative has an end goal in mind—like they know exactly what they want. But in reality, that's not true. Part of art is the iteration, so making sure the tools are able to handle that level of iteration ends up being extremely important. This also makes it very hard to benchmark these things, because their optimum isn't grounded in their head.

Zach： For us, it's a bit specific to photos. We do a lot on identity preservation, identity consistency. We set out to do image editing of real photos that people want to preserve the identity in. What surprised me is that people are actually very willing to generate a new image—put themselves in different environments, generate AI headshots, generate AI video of themselves using Photo's technology. That surprised me a little bit. And people are getting very creative with that.

Q： The video use case is actually a surprising one. Can you explain it further?

Zach： Right now our technology mostly works on images. But people are getting creative. They say: "Oh, the video models are so bad at identity preservation. What I can do is use Photo to generate frames of those videos, and then use a video model to take those frames into a video, so that identity is good." That's something we didn't think of at the beginning—but they figured out, creators.

Matt： I've seen it multiple times now where people find very interesting ways to use tools—not just our tools, but our tools in addition to other tools that you never expect to work. For every reason it shouldn't work, but people find crazy ways to get these things to work.

六、工具抽象：给人用 vs. 给 agent 用

Q： When it comes to tool abstraction—I almost see the end user as a type of agent who needs to use tools. There are tools for humans and tools for agents. How do you see those differ?

Matt： It's interesting to look at how creative tools have existed traditionally. You look at Photoshop, Illustrator, Blender—whatever modality you're interested in—and if you hop into those programs, they're extremely complex. You can literally have a whole career learning the ins and outs. Where agents come along, and the ability to have agents work with tools, is then the ability to tune the tool to the user. Some users care about relighting an image or removing a background. Some want extreme levels of control. So the question is: how do you interact with an agent that can choose that level of abstraction that makes sense for you?

Zach： Creativity is about directing something to achieve your creative mind. This directing thing is so important. That's where the agent comes into place. It's not about mastering those tools—it's about directing an agent who can use those tools to achieve your creativity.

Another lesson we learned in photography: a lot of people edit photos, but a lot of people just don't want to edit photos at all. They have creativity in mind, they just don't want to do the work, or they're too busy. But they still have a creative mind. So if you have some kind of agent that understands your creativity and can express it without much effort from you—these passive agents that run in the background—I feel like that's even better.

七、下一代界面：人与 agent 共用

Q： First we had simplistic tools where users prompt and get an image—one shot. Later we got crazy nodes and concrete workflows. Then canvases. So how do you think about the next interface for both agents and humans?

Matt： I think it's false to assume every user is going to want the same thing. You look at directors of movies—some go super deep into the weeds, making sure every glass is in the right place. Others don't care about any of that; all they care about is "is the actor doing the line in the right way?" I think all that is true in these creative tools too. So you have to create an agent that can work with these different directors—one that has the ability to manipulate things at very fine-grained levels, and one that's more on the literary side and just wants to talk and interact and have fun with the tool.

Zach： Personalization is absolutely key here. Different people have different levels of acceptance of complexity, and different preferences and tastes. The agent needs to understand the user really well and be flexible. It also needs really good memory—if I tell you once, I shouldn't need to tell you again in the future.

Q： If you travel down the abstraction layers far enough, you can build anything with C and a compiler. But it would be weird to assume agents are going to rebuild creative tools every time. Somewhere you have to draw a line in the sand. Where is that layer?

Matt： I don't know if I have a concrete answer because it's constantly changing. There are arguments for different underlying representations. Is it a 3D world you're trying to model? Images? Are you trying to break up images into layers? I'm not sure we've really hit on, as an industry, what the right representation is.

Zach： There's the abstraction of what's a model and what's a tool. A tool can be a little more than a model. A model can do a lot of things that a lot of traditional tools used to do. I personally think models are replacing a lot of the old tools for not-so-precise control. But at the same time, people are putting more precise control back into the model. So it's a back-and-forth process.

八、如何评估"好"：从指标到品味

Q： How do you think about what "good" looks like when it comes to generation?

Matt： Being a researcher, there's always a focus on how to convert "good" into a metric you can measure, so agents and research teams can optimize against it. However, there's no way that will ever tell the full story. You have to involve humans in the loop during development—bring in creatives and people whose taste you're trying to mimic. But that also can't be the full story, because one person creating one video is going to have a different vision than someone else. This is where personalization really becomes key.

Q： Can you measure taste?

Matt： I think you can measure taste given some constraint. Given a population, you can measure the taste of that population. You can also measure the taste of an individual user. Depending on how coarse you get, it becomes more muddled.

Zach： For us, there's evaluation before you ship and evaluation after you ship. With identity preservation, we really care about each individual user saying: are they happy with the result? It's very hard for us to come up with a universal metric, especially for subjective things like taste, and even for very personal things like identity. We always ask the person themselves, or someone close to them, to evaluate identity.

After shipping, especially for subjective things, that's so important for personalization. Have we made the user happy? Can you collect that feedback in an ambient, non-intrusive way? That's just so important.

Q： I was talking to someone earlier about their photo result. His view was: happiness and truth-seeking are two different things. When he saw the result he said "this is exactly me, the likeness is 100%—but I look too fat." So he's not happy. How are you guys solving that?

Zach： At the end of the day, the user is the one in control. If they want to look a certain way in the photo, that's their choice and we need to empower them. Beautification has been a long-time debate. Everyone has their own taste. Personalization is key.

九、长尾用例与隐藏需求

Q： When you launch a tool, there's a distribution of use cases. Are there specific use cases you wanted to move from the fat tail to the head?

Matt： One interesting one was seeing how brands would bring in brand guidelines. It's not something we considered very much, but once they brought them in, you realize this was an extremely useful source of information for getting to what a user is interested in.

Zach： Our current API only supports humans and pets for identity. There were some users trying to hack our system to use it for personalization for product photography. They were like: "We want this." So now that's on our roadmap.

Q： How do you see the difference between human likeness and product likeness?

Zach： There are similarities. Everybody says, "Oh, the general foundation model is already very good at identity"—until you try to generate an image of yourself. Same thing with product photography: it's pretty good until you're the product owner trying to generate a photo of that particular product. It just stops working.

The difference: identity is more subjective and dynamic—it's really hard to put into words. With products, you need to get the text right, the shape right. Text rendering is so important in product photography, not so much in identity preservation.

十、控制力（Controllability）

Q： Phase one of AI: we generate things and keep reprompting—almost like a lottery. Phase two: we put more control either at the model level or the system level. What does it mean for an AI creative tool to have better controllability?

Matt： It's an open question, but the short answer is you definitely need to add control. That was the number one complaint from anyone trying to use these tools professionally: you don't want to operate with text. There has to be something more than text. Most recently we've done a lot of work in video-to-video because that's a great way to start incorporating pretty precise controls—in time, in space, scribble, point to certain regions. We're adding more of these controls on top of the model, both for users and so agents can be instructed to do these edits.

Zach： It's also about helping the user say what they want. Often the model doesn't do exactly what you want because the user's prompt is ambiguous. The under-exploited research direction is: if the model can tell you what it needs from you—ask you for inputs before it does something. Today it's almost a one-way street: the user tells the model what to do.

When you work with a creative professional as an amateur, they ask you questions. You tell them your opinions, preferences, and they achieve those for you.

Matt： That's a really good point. If you go to a studio and say "make me a 10-second video about a dog jumping in the grass," they're never going to take that deal. They're going to want more specific. You have to figure out how to apply that in AI.

十一、模型与应用的协同设计

Q： Does model-app co-design have benefits when it comes to introducing more controllability?

Matt： I think so. Being able to have users interact in your app and work through their workflow, you learn which things a user actually cares about—which steps they have to redo multiple times, why they chose what they chose. That can help a lot with model design.

Zach： The other direction is also important: your product can help educate the user on the best way to use the model. We're seeing that with coding—people used to do everything in VS Code, and now Claude Code in the terminal can be the best way to use a model because you cut through a lot of other things. App and model co-design has that advantage too.

十二、无头工具与视觉表征

Q： When we think about AI creative tools, the first thing that comes to mind is they're very visual—we're visual animals. Now we're used to working with agents. Is there a world where creative tools become primarily headless?

Matt： Going back to working with a studio: you're talking to an agent, you're talking to someone who's working with you. But they're not talking back to you in text. They're showing you examples. "Do you like these ideas?" I think that's the direction AI creative tools will go.

Zach： When I try to brainstorm something—if you write code to generate an HTML page showing the graphics, I just want a sketch. An image is more than a thousand words. On the other hand, people haven't figured out the best way to feed visual information to a model. Models today are a little bit blind. They're getting better at high-level semantic understanding, but visual is very redundant information—pixels are redundant, unlike language. How do you give the model that information so it's not blind anymore? That's so important, and I don't think we've figured it out yet.

Q： This reminds me of the importance of the right representation. Matt, you worked on Nerf, and before that people were on meshes, now Gaussian Splat. How do you think about representations?

Matt： The representation question ultimately comes down to the control question too. What do you want to be able to modify in the future? If you're designing a poster and you'll be changing the text, you probably want a representation that's not pixel level. If you're just modifying pictures, then pixels make sense. If you're navigating 3D scenes for movies, you may want a 3D representation. There's no one answer—it's whatever makes sense with the given level of control.

Zach： Right now a lot of people focus on what's the best representation for the model. But the consumer of those representations is not just the model—it's also the human. Can you represent it in a way the human can understand and control?

十三、作为艺术家做研究：直觉的来源

Q： How do you develop the intuition for building for creators as former researchers? Matt, I know you're also an artist.

Matt： It gives you some insight on the control aspect. One of the first things drilled in during our classes when I was younger was the blank canvas problem. There's a tendency of getting stuck by that, then starting very meticulously, doing one section at a time. But in reality, it's better to just go at it, try things out, mess with it until you get to the thing you're ultimately happy with. AI tools are no different. The iteration cycle is fast, and you really want to iterate—it's not just put in a single prompt and get a final answer.

Another aspect is figuring out what language to use to describe what works or doesn't work in the tool—because this can be very important for evaluation. Throughout art history, people have built ways to describe composition, lighting, etc. Having that knowledge and incorporating it into the design of the models can be very helpful.

Zach： Talking to users is really important. When you ask the user how they want to improve a photo, they don't know. When you make some edit, now they have opinions on whether they like your edits or not. They know what they don't like. So talking to users is really important. And having a very good understanding of the technology so you can stay a little ahead of users is also important.

十四、过程 vs. 结果

Q： In art, if a human is creating, a lot goes into the process. The final result is when you decide the process has come to a point of your satisfaction. How do you compare that with how agents create art?

Matt： In many ways it can be quite similar, and even when agents create art, there's still the human involved—because that process is the part that's fun for artists. Look at VFX breakdowns: you see how many different steps and layers went into the final pixels. People who do that work find it very exciting. The way things break down in the future with AI tools will look very different, but it'll still exist—a back-and-forth between agents doing some parts of the work and humans tying it all together.

Zach： There's always a debate: is regular photography going to die? I don't think so. There are a lot of people doing photography not just because they enjoy the result—they enjoy the process so much. Even though digital cameras have become so good, many people still take film. Film has this unique thing: you have to capture that moment, otherwise it's gone, and you don't see the result right away—you have to develop the film. People still enjoy those processes.

At the same time, there are people who just want the results. How do you empower them with AI creative tools to unlock their creativity? That's where I'm excited about AI tooling for creativity.

十五、潜在需求与新进入者

Q： There's a lot of latent demand—people who didn't have money for a fancy camera, never took a film-developing class. New entrants on the market often target users who were not users of the original tool. Where have you observed that latent demand come from in photography?

Zach： Photography is not just art—it's also a document of our important moments and life. A lot of people want a good recording of their moments and lives. They may not have the skills or the equipment. In the past they tried to buy the latest iPhone, hoping for a better camera, but they don't have editing skills. Those are the latent demands we try to address—whether through AI models or AI agents, creative tools that make it easy or even do the job for them, so they can get good photos of their important moments.

Q： At our firm we had headshot photo shoots—photographer, studio, lighting, makeup. Recently more folks just say "I couldn't make it, but I'm going to generate it on Photo." There's an interesting market segment where you need physical presence to take a photo. Have you seen that?

Zach： Yes. And a photo shoot is a set-up environment anyway—you're setting up lighting, you go there. Most of the time you don't have full control because of physical limits. When you do it generatively, there are so many more things you can do—change the lighting, change the angle, even change your makeup.

Q： Matt, you take a lot of photos. How do you think about the old vs. new world of AI photography?

Matt： It's fun. There's the overlap where you can do them together. But there's also where you treat it as a very different medium. When you do things purely in the AI world, you can think about what an image would look like halfway across the world, or in specific studio lighting situations you would never actually be able to do. I find myself sometimes seeing a cool image and trying to think: how would I actually replicate this in AI? What are the right terms to use? You go down a rabbit hole. It unlocks a new area that I haven't been able to explore before.

十六、传统工具的未来

Q： How do you think about the role of traditional tools in the new world of AI creative tools? Are they becoming infrastructure where agents know how to use them, or are they getting rebuilt?

Matt： All these things are just tools. Whoever has a vision and wants to execute on it—what tool is right for them? For some people, it'll always be Photoshop. For a newer generation, it won't be. Maybe it'll be agents interacting with these tools. I think these things will always exist to some extent, but find different uses by different users.

Zach： It's not a new problem. Tools have always been iterating. There's always a new tool coming out, whether AI-based or not. We're probably at a stage where tools are iterating so fast. Some tools may not be needed in the future—they'll be replaced. But a lot will still exist. Even the AI models we have today will be replaced. It's just an iterative process.

十七、自有模型与开源的价值

Q： What's the benefit of having your own model? Is there a benefit to open-sourcing the model?

Matt： One of the biggest benefits of having your own model is the ability to build off of it. Your product has a certain set of users and you want to do the best for them. The best way is by customizing—making adjustments to the model that don't make sense for a general open-source model but make sense for your product. That's not always easy on top of existing models, and may require specialization only you can provide.

Zach： For us, a little different. We don't directly do foundation models—we mostly work on personalization. There are so many cool companies like Luma. We really think personalization is something the user should own. That's the user's model. You build a user's model for them, but they have full control. We want to build the technology to enable them to do that, and let them combine it with any foundation model they like—a specialized model for a particular use case, for example. Disentangling personalization vs. foundation is the goal we have.

十八、未来几年，最好的艺术家与其他人的差距

Q： With advances in AI tools, what will separate the best artists from everyone else in the next couple years?

Matt： From my experience already, it's very obvious. When people use our tools—or any AI tools—you can tell pretty quickly someone who's just throwing in a prompt versus someone who's thought more about the whole holistic story. I get amazed at what very creative artists can do with these tools. Many times you see things that don't seem like they would have been possible. That's the thing that separates it: how do you make something unique with the tools you have access to now?

Zach： It's hard to say there's a good artist and bad artist—but probably good artists and average artists. AI tools are definitely raising the lower bar, so even someone like me can create something pretty heat with the tools. But I also think the gap between the very best and the average is going to be even bigger. Because, as Matt said, it's so much easier for people to express creativity when they have a very good understanding of what they want. They can achieve something they couldn't before. So the gap will get bigger. And of course, they need a very good understanding of all these new AI tools as well.

Matt： One final point. You've seen AI generations that are bad—we've all seen them. I can guarantee the same tools that made those bad generations have also made generations you would think are amazing.

十九、AI = slop？还是用户的镜子？

Q： Something I heard from someone today was: they assume AI to be slop. Anything AI models make would be slop. It's up to the humans to create something out of it. If you just say "I want a picture of a cup of coffee," the first generation is mediocre. It's nothing unique, nothing personal. So it really is up to the user to make it theirs—to put their mark and style on it.

What are your thoughts on personal styles—what constitutes style, what constitutes personalization for users?

Zach： That's such a hard question. I almost feel like there's no good way to define it other than you can learn it from data. It's not just the user's existing data—it's also: when they use it, what's their reaction? Even if you ask the user what their own style is, they can put some keywords there but it's not complete.

If the way to solve the problem is to really define their style and then model their style—that's probably not the approach I would take, even from a research perspective. Look at the data, find the attribution the data shows you, and then you've probably figured out that style. And even then, it's still implicit because it's part of the model.

Matt： I think that's also something that changes over time. Both as a person changes—the stuff I thought was interesting 5 years ago vs. now is very different. And, you know, I'm working on one project one week and another project another week, and I inevitably need a different style. Maybe that's how you define styles for specific tasks, and personalization is sort of the holistic what you as a user tend to like. But yeah, I think it's a bit mixed.

收尾

主持人： Awesome. And with that—well, thank you so much for coming on the pod. This is such a fun discussion. Really appreciate it.

嘉宾： Yeah. Thank you for having us.

观看原视频 →
item.description:
## 一、最反直觉的洞察：AI 不是在杀死摄影，而是在把"决定性瞬间"从快门时刻**搬迁**到后期
布列松那句神圣的"决定性瞬间"统治了摄影 80 年——它的本质是：你必须在快门按下的 1/125 秒里完成所有创造性决策，因为**之后就什么都做不了了**。Zach 在对话里轻描淡写地说出了一句颠覆性的话："摄影师在 post-capture 阶段比以前更有乐趣了。"
这句话的真正含义是：**摄影的"创造时间窗口"正在从毫秒级被拉长到无限**。这不是工具升级，而是介质的相变——就像电影从胶片走向数字时，剪辑从"物理切割"变成"非线性"，整个创作的时间结构被重写了。摄影师现在面对的不再是"瞬间艺术"，而是一种类似**雕塑或绘画**的"持续可塑介质"。这是一次悄无声息的范式革命，多数人还没意识到。
## 二、跨领域连接：AI 创作工具正在重演**编程语言抽象层的百年历史**——但跳过了中间步骤
听 Matt 谈"应该把抽象层画在哪条线上"，我立刻想到的是：从汇编 → C → Python → 自然语言编程，软件业花了 70 年才走到"用英语写程序"。而 AI 创作工具正在 3 年内压

item.title: [Business of Sport] From £25m in Debt to £15m in the Bank: How The Hundred Saved Yorkshire
item.dc:creator: Business of Sport
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、最反直觉的发现：拯救约克郡的，不是板球，而是"自我背叛"

整个故事最惊人的悖论在于：一家有 165 年历史的会员制俱乐部，必须先"背叛自己的身份"才能活下去。 Colin 反复强调"不要害怕去会员化"——这句话翻译过来其实是：你赖以为荣的那个组织形式，正是把你两次推到破产边缘的元凶。

更深一层的反讽是：救命的 1 亿英镑并非来自板球本身，而是来自一个被传统球迷骂作"不是板球"的新产品（The Hundred）。也就是说，约克郡是被它最看不起的那种板球救活的。这构成一种近乎黑色幽默的系统性真相——一个组织最深的护城河，往往就是它最大的负债表。

二、跨领域连接：板球的"第五天问题" = SaaS 的"长尾用户"陷阱

Colin 关于"五日制测试赛第五天所有人都亏钱"的论断，几乎是一个完美的单位经济学案例。第五天的存在不是产品特性，是历史惯性形成的成本黑洞——票价压到 10 镑只为填场子，运营、转播、餐饮全员亏损。

这跟 SaaS 行业里"保留那 3% 重度免费用户却消耗 40% 服务器成本"是同一个结构：当某个尾部环节的边际收益持续为负，继续提供它本身就是对核心用户的隐性补贴。 把测试赛改为四日制，本质上和 SaaS 公司"sunset 免费版"是同一种决策——但板球用了几十年都没人敢按那个按钮，因为传统的情感价格远高于财务价格。

再往外推一层：英超 vs EFL、Premier League vs County Cricket、好莱坞 vs 长尾流媒体——所有"金字塔型生态"都面临同一道数学题：顶端的繁荣是不是真在反哺底座，还是只是借底座的合法性收租？

三、最被低估的洞察：约克郡其实在做一场"国家失灵"的私人补救

Colin 顺口说出的一句话值得放大：澳大利亚的悉尼、佩斯球场是州政府和联邦政府盖的，俱乐部一分钱不用出；而英国所有板球场都是俱乐部自己筹钱建的。

这意味着 The Hundred 的 1 亿英镑、去会员化、私人投资者——整套金融工程其实是在替缺席的英国公共体育基建买单。 约克郡不是在"商业化"，它在做一件本该由国家做的事：用资本市场的工具，补一个公共物品的窟窿。

这就解释了一个表面矛盾：为什么一个 32 岁的主持人会觉得自己已经"老"了，而 Colin 却把希望寄托在 10 年后的青少年身上。因为这不是一场关于球的赌博，是一场关于"谁来替英国体育文化付固定成本"的代际接力。 当板球俱乐部开始像主权基金一样思考——成立投资子公司、设 10 年战略、把比赛日变成科技园区式综合体——它已经悄悄从"体育组织"变成了"地方文化的资产管理人"。

约克郡如果赌赢，模板就会复制到 Sussex、Kent；如果赌输，英国可能会失去的不只是一个俱乐部，而是一种"传统机构能否在不靠政府的情况下自我现代化"的可能性证明。这才是这场 2500 万债务到 1500 万现金故事真正的赌注。

📝 转录文本

开场：一个未完成的使命

"We could sit there and do nothing, not being unkind. I could put my coat on, walk out the door and say, 'Right, I've sorted it all out. You got 15 million quid in the bank. Everything's hunky dory,' and walk away from it. I don't want to do that. I want to finish the mission, what I started in 2002. But this is for the good of Yorkshire cricket for the next 50 years. That's why we're doing it."

"Don't be fearful of demutualization. You've been a members club for 160 odd years, but as a members club, we've virtually nearly gone bankrupt twice."

"I would change test match cricket to 4 day. The fifth day in a test match, everybody loses money. The ground loses money, the broadcaster loses money. It's no good for anybody."

"We created The Hundred where we believe that would be an attractive investment for investors. The total investment for The Hundred — if you sold every team — it was nearly a billion pound. Without that, to be honest, that saved the game certainly for the next 10 years."

"I went back to Yorkshire in February 2024 and the debts had spiraled up to 25 million again. They even had an Ashes test match during that time and they still lost money. I still can't get my head around that. If you have an Ashes test match, you should make between three and five million pounds net profit a year. Full stop."

"I find business fascinating."

一、欢迎与背景：板球当下的混乱图景

主持人： Colin, welcome to the Business of Sport. It's really great to have you in the studio.

Colin： Yeah, thanks very much for the invitation, and uh, looking forward to the chat.

主持人： Well, me too. You know, I know I'm the one that's meant to be hosting this, but how on earth do we even begin to look at cricket at the moment? It is a chaotic landscape, really from top to bottom. You are in position at Yorkshire as chair at the moment. Tell us a little bit, just to give some of the context of the challenges that you're facing.

Colin： Basically, I've been in professional cricket now since 2002, when I first got involved with Yorkshire when they were in financial distress for the first time. And then I got back involved with Yorkshire two years ago, 2024, when again they were in their second financial distress. So that's how I come back involved with them.

And yeah, really, I think cricket is in an interesting place at the present time. I think there's some massive challenges ahead at all levels in cricket — international cricket, county cricket, one day cricket, however you look at it. I think there's going to be a lot of challenges in the next 10 years, and the whole game has got to look at that very seriously and how we handle that going forward, because the way people are digesting cricket and following cricket and watching cricket is changing rapidly.

You've only got to look at — even test matches in India, the audiences of test matches in India is dropping. People don't realize that. We're fortunate in England that test matches, we're still sellout grounds when we do have in England international matches, which is great. But that might not last forever. That could change if the audience's views and demands change in the future.

二、世代差异：板球观众正在分裂

主持人： I didn't realize that test matches were declining in India. That's really interesting. Do you think we're in an era of too much?

Colin： I think the problem that we've got in cricket — you can say it's a problem, you can say it's an opportunity — but I think the older audiences like test match cricket. They've been brought up on test match cricket. The younger audiences, different lifestyle, different demands, like one day cricket. You have a result one way or another. It's quick, it's fast, it's entertaining. The younger audiences find test matches very boring.

So if you look at the audience at test matches and then compare that with the audience especially at a Hundred match — totally different audience.

主持人： Would you say 32 is young?

Colin： From where I'm sitting today? Yeah.

主持人： Okay. I love test cricket, but even at 32 I've grown up with it being the primary part of cricket. If you're talking about the real birth of franchise cricket from 2007 IPL onwards — it was the moments in the '05 Ashes for someone around my generation. But now we'll start to see very quickly the first part of the generation that have grown up with the IPL being one of the first things they've watched, with the short form games being one of the first things they watch. It'll be really interesting to see how they prioritize and want to consume and participate in cricket.

Colin： And that is the challenge we've got in cricket for the next 10 years: how do we handle that change? How do we keep the interest watching test match cricket from that younger generation at 32 or even younger? That is a challenge we've all got.

三、改革测试赛：把五日改成四日

主持人： What would you do?

Colin： The main thing — and I said this 10 years ago, maybe I was ahead of my time — I would change test match cricket to 4-day from 5-day. I think 5 days is far too long anyway. Not many go to 5 days when you look at it.

主持人： No, particularly with England.

Colin： Exactly. So if you look at 4-day test match cricket, you play 90 overs a day, it's out at 10:30 in the morning, and you make it more watchable and attractive from a younger generation's point of view.

The other thing we've got to look at in test matches is who's going to play test match cricket going forward. You've seen it over the last two months — you had New Zealand playing Ireland, and you had India playing Afghanistan. Both of those finished within three days and were won by an innings and a lot of runs from a top-quality test match team to a relatively new test match team.

So how many teams are going to be playing test match cricket in 10 years' time? I don't think we'll have as many as we have today. Because what's the point of playing test match cricket if you've got nobody watching it and you're losing money on staging it? All those challenges have got to come through ICC and all the countries who play test match cricket.

主持人： And it's about creating the product for the fans, but it's also about creating the product that makes sense for business as well. Because if you have a fifth day that virtually never gets played, but you're still selling tickets, you're still bringing in all of the food and beverage, the staffing that you're putting on for a fifth day…

Colin： The fifth day in a test match, everybody loses money. The ground loses money, the broadcaster loses money. It's no good for anybody. When you do have a fifth day, you virtually let the crowd in for nothing — £10 a ticket or something like that — just to try and fill the ground as much as possible, because you never pre-sell the fifth day anyway. So the fifth day financially is a negative.

四、传统体育的抗拒与"The Hundred"的诞生

主持人： So you brought that up 10 years ago. We joke a lot on the show about the challenge of big change in sports that we would class as traditional. If you get pushback like that — if things don't change off the back of it — is it because there is just a resilience to doing things, is it because there's a challenge in shifting the mentality that something that has always been should always be?

Colin： I think there's a time and a place for change. When I brought it up 10 years ago, we had other challenges at the ECB and within English cricket. So at that point in time, it wasn't paramount.

The big change that we looked at then was The Hundred — how to bring in a new tournament to attract a new audience. We did all the research and we found that we had a million people each year that watched cricket in one format or another. Problem is, it was the same million people that just got churned around every year. So we weren't attracting new audiences.

When we went out and did the research, we found out that we didn't have families, we didn't have women, we didn't have children. And we asked the question, "Why aren't you watching cricket?" And it came back: goes on too long, difficult to understand the scoreboard, etc.

So when we came out with The Hundred, we came out with a new game that was easy to understand, shorter format, finished in 2 and 1/2 hours. 100 balls, runs, wickets, and a conversation. That's all you need to understand about it.

And the other big thing we did — we got it back on terrestrial television, which helped a hell of a lot with the BBC. So that was the challenge at the time: how do you attract that new audience and increase the audience? Which we did and we got it through and we succeeded with.

为什么 Blast 不够？

主持人： What were the responses of the individuals that you spoke to in that type of survey as to why maybe something like the Blast wasn't servicing them in the way the Hundred has been able to?

Colin： I think the Blast — it sounds horrible saying this, but it's fact — I think the Blast turned into a male social event where the guys would go out on a Friday night or over a weekend with their mates and guys from work. So it turned into a very social male-dominated event, not a family event. And that's where we lost out on the Blast as it was, and still is to be honest.

五、商业逻辑：从产品到投资标的

主持人： Let's duck into the — you brought it up, so let's run with it. There is the fan product element. Now, it's easy to say there's also been a very strong business element. When you set out, how did you position both of those two together? Was the objective early on to recognize this was a really good chance to inject some much-needed capital back into English cricket if it worked?

Colin： Yeah, it was one of the first things we always said — what the game needed was investment coming into the game. And it's still there. That is still a challenge certainly at county level.

So from that point of view, we created The Hundred where we believe that would be an attractive investment for investors to look at in the future. How long that would take, we didn't know at that point in time, but we knew it would be attractive to investors.

I remember standing up when we got it through and we launched it, and I said then: "In five years' time, we could have investors from India, America and all the rest of it to look at investing in The Hundred." And people laughed at me and said, "You must be joking. That's never going to happen. Indians are never going to invest in The Hundred in England." And I got slated for getting The Hundred on the table and coming through. But that was our vision in 2017.

不急着卖：先证明价值

主持人： How important was it to not sell it at the beginning? You create a new tournament — and have investors in from day one. How important was it to prove there was value in that product?

Colin： Yeah, very important. There's no way we ever wanted to do that. The plan was always to run it for five years. Get the audience, get the figures, get everything up and running, get the broadcaster behind us — which we did with Sky, they've been brilliant. So that was always the plan: 5 to 10 years to have something that you can take to market, whatever format that looked like.

主持人： And for those that challenged it, was it the fact that there was already so much cricket they didn't want it to conflict with other formats?

Colin： I think the ones who challenged it virtually were in a very comfortable position with their own clubs and counties. They were profitable, they were successful on the field, they had a good business behind them. So they saw no need to bring anything else to the table. They were happy with what they had.

But it was the other counties who were saying, "Hold on a minute. We need something new. We need something to bring more investment into the game, to bring more capital into the game. How do we do it?" So it took us two and a half years to get it through, but we got there in the end.

六、起源故事：沙漠之春那个早晨

主持人： When you say "get it through," do you mean two and a half years just from idea to approval, where you can then go, right, okay, we're going to create the teams, start selling the product?

Colin： Exactly. It was 2 and a half years from the initial idea, which Sanjay Patel brought to the table with Andrew Strauss, Tom Harrison and myself — sat us down and told us about this idea of The Hundred — to when we actually got the counties to vote for it. Because he had to take it to the stakeholders to get it through and get the 75% majority, which we achieved. That was a 2 and 1/2 year time scale of constantly talking to the stakeholders, updating them where we were, all the ideas we were having. We had meeting after meeting after meeting to get over the line.

主持人： So Sanjay one day was just like, "This is — I've had an idea"?

Colin： Well, when he presented to us, I'll never forget it. We were in Spain. We went to look at Desert Springs — do we buy a ground in Desert Springs for going across there in winter? That was where we started off. And Sanjay said, "Right, Saturday morning, I want to see you at 10:00. I've got an idea I want to throw at you three."

He got a room in the hotel. He presented this new idea of The Hundred to us, and I'll never forget. We all looked at each other and said, "Really? How does that work?" And slowly, bit by bit, for three months, he then virtually put some flesh on the bones of what it looked like and how it would work, etc. And yeah, we all signed into it very quickly.

主持人： In that first conversation, was there a change in format — i.e. shorter overs?

Colin： Yes. From day one, it was 100 balls, and it was not six-ball overs. From day one, that never changed.

主持人： I can imagine you guys sitting there going, "Well…"

Colin： We all said, "How does that fit in with the averages, how does that fit in with your career record and all that sort of stuff?" — which is the first thing a cricketer thinks about. And he said, "Don't worry about all that. We'll get all that sorted, etc., and we'll make it work and we'll fit it in with MCC laws and regulations." And yeah, we did all that. But he never changed it from day one.

七、新观众的到来与"纯粹派"的反对
