I'm curious on your side — I know that YC generally does not fund solo founders and there were only six solo founders in your batch. Why make the exception?

Yeah, it's definitely rare. First it's like — actually it has to be technical. Then I think as a solo founder you need to be able to both build and sell, and it's easier to find people who can kind of do the building part and then learn to do the sales part during the batch — like as you did — than people who can do the sales part but are going to have to learn to build. So that's the first big-picture criteria I look at.

But then in your case in particular, it was just such exceptionally strong founder-market fit. Even back — I mean it was early days, like you mentioned — it wasn't completely clear kind of where things would land and what the specific idea would hone in on. But it was just clear that you had a very unique set of expertise and skills that would transfer over in interesting ways.

So I think — combination of being able to build, having a real valuable domain expertise. And I just — it just seemed like you were going to do this either way. I think that's another thing — sometimes you see solo founders and it's like you just need an extra level of grit and determination to do the thing. And you can just get a sense for when you're interviewing or talking to people. Some people, I feel like if we don't fund them, they're just kind of going to go back to school, go back to their job, or they're going to hang around and not get much done. But with you, it definitely felt like — this is going to happen either way, so we just would like to be a part of it.

That's awesome. Well, I think that was an accurate read because definitely at the time I was like — I'm going to build this either way.

Great. The train is leaving the station.

Yeah, that's actually the exact phrase we use internally — like if it feels like the train is leaving the station, then you just want to work with those people. And so sometimes it's like they're a single founder. Sometimes they're still in college. We don't really, despite the perception on Twitter maybe, we don't actually like finding people who are still in college and they're going to drop out. But every now and again you meet people who just — it's happening either way. So you look for those people and then you back them.

Koval 的产品路线图

I'm sure another big part of this round is going to be investing in the product and the product roadmap. What are some of the things you're personally most excited about that's on the Koval product roadmap for the next year?

Yeah, I mean, I think as everything goes more agentic — how do you build evals? That's the big question. Doing it well — it's easy to build a script that kind of goes in a loop and iterates on your agent. To actually build a self-improving agent or systems that help do reinforcement learning or prompt optimization or just optimization of your system — that's really hard.

And so we're looking at how do we build all of these systems across the board to help you understand exactly where things are going wrong, get really high signal out of it, and then do something about that.

And then I think the biggest next stage for us is just how do we take what's worked for many Fortune 500 enterprises, for some of the fastest growing startups and hyperscalers in AI, and replicate that to every Fortune 500 that's building voice agents.

给未来创始人的忠告

Cool. Alright, well I'm excited to see it happen. Maybe just to close out — for everyone watching who want to start a company themselves one day, any sort of closing bits of advice for them?

Yeah, I think just being really obsessive is one of the most useful things — and being really curious. I thought that there was a way to become ready to be a startup founder, but really you just have to jump into it and be really curious and work as hard as you possibly can. You can make up for a lot of shortcomings by just working harder and moving faster than everyone else.

That is shockingly true. Cool. Well, I think that's all we have time for today. Congratulations again on raising the Series A, Brooke, and we're excited to see where Koval goes from here.

Thanks so much, Har, for all your support.

观看原视频 →
item.description:
## 一、最反直觉的洞察：语音 Agent 不是"替代人"，而是"重新定义需求曲线"
大部分人讨论语音 Agent，焦点都在"裁掉客服"——这是一个**减法叙事**。但 Brooke 透露的真正秘密是一个**加法叙事**：当航空公司客服永远秒接、永远懂你的上下文，你会开始打**从前根本不会打的电话**——"我提前到机场了，有没有早一班？"这种需求过去因为"打电话太麻烦"被压抑了。
这意味着语音 Agent 真正的市场不是"现有呼叫中心的人力成本"，而是**被等待时间杀死的潜在交互总量**。这是一个隐藏的、远比"替代人工"大一个数量级的市场——就像 Uber 不是抢出租车生意，而是创造了"我本来不会出门"的那些出行。
## 二、跨领域连接：自动驾驶的"万圣节小孩"如何变成 Fortune 500 的合规护城河
这是整段访谈里最优雅的跨域同构——而且 Brooke 自己只点到一半。
Whimo 的数据集哲学是：**1 亿英里里那个穿万圣节服装的小孩，比 9999 万英里的平均路况更重要**。普通 ML 看平均准确率，自动驾驶看长尾极端样本。
把这个范式直接平移到语音 Agent

item.title: [Sequoia Capital] Memory and Continual Learning: Engram's Dan Biderman and Jessy Lin
item.dc:creator: Sequoia Capital
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、最反直觉的观察：KV Cache 是"硅基大脑的胎记"

Dan 在访谈中埋了一个极具张力的数字对比，但他自己没有把它推到最深处——让我替他完成这次顿悟：

一篇 Taylor Swift 的维基百科条目（几十 KB 文本），在 70B Llama 的 KV cache 里要膨胀成 约 80 GB 的 HBM 状态；而整个 70B 模型的权重也才约 100 GB，里面"压缩"了几乎整个互联网。

这意味着：模型的"长期记忆"压缩比是其"工作记忆"压缩比的 10⁶ 量级。 换句话说，今天的 LLM 是一个有着博士级长期记忆、却用整面墙的便利贴来记今天对话的怪物。所谓"上下文工程"，本质是在用胶带把这个不对称结构补起来。Engram 真正在做的，不是"加一层记忆"，而是修复硅基智能与生俱来的代谢失衡——把短期昂贵的 KV 状态，逐步代谢成长期廉价的权重状态。

这恰好对应生物学中的 "记忆固化"（memory consolidation）：海马体中昂贵、高保真、易丢失的短时记忆，在睡眠期间被慢慢翻译成皮层中廉价、抽象、持久的长时记忆。Engram 实际上是在给 LLM 装一个"夜班翻译官"。

二、跨领域连接：Engram 是"反 Snowflake"——一个把数据库重新溶解回算法的公司

过去 30 年软件工程的根本信条是"算法与数据分离"：Codd 的关系代数让数据可以被独立查询、独立审计、独立迁移。Snowflake、Databricks、Oracle 整个万亿美金市值的版图，建立在"数据是名词、算法是动词"这一形而上学之上。

而 Dan 不经意间说了一句几乎是异端的话：

"深度学习的魔法，是把数据库和算法揉在了一起，于是 Anthropic 的可解释性团队得花大力气再把它们拆开。"

Engram 的赌注，是这种"揉在一起"不仅不是 bug，而是下一代基础设施的形态： - Snowflake 的世界里，公司知识 = 表 + SQL； - Engram 的世界里，公司知识 = 权重 + 提示。

更妙的是，Dan 在最后一段亲口承认了这个隐喻——他说 Engram 想做的是"对数据平面的神经接口"，类比 Databricks 和 Oracle。这是一种把过去 40 年关系代数的胜利有选择地撤销的尝试。如果他们对了，未来不再是"模型查询数据库"，而是"数据库被慢慢吸收进模型的突触里"。这是数据架构史上一次哲学反转。

三、隐藏的系统性真相：他们其实在卖"组织无意识"

注意两位创始人反复用的一个比喻——"梦"。Dan 说："给模型时间从真实交互中撤回，去试验自己的可供性（affordances）。" 这听起来浪漫，但藏着一个非常冷峻的系统级判断：

当代知识工作的瓶颈，不是"模型不够聪明"，也不是"上下文不够长"，而是组织的隐性知识从未被写下来。Notion 文档里写的是 what，Slack 里漂的是 who，Harvey 里跑的是 how——但"我们公司为什么这么做事"这一层从来没有载体。前沿模型再大，也读不到一份不存在的文档。

Engram 的真正产品，因此不是"记忆"也不是"持续学习"，而是 "组织无意识的物化层"（a substrate for organizational unconscious）。LoRA 权重在这里扮演的角色，类似于荣格说的"集体无意识"——不在任何一个文档里，但弥漫在所有交互的统计结构中。

这也解释了一个看似矛盾的现象：Jesse 说她不希望 ChatGPT 把她的工作记忆和生活记忆混在一起。这不是产品 UX 偏好，而是揭示了一个深层真相——记忆的价值不在于完整，而在于边界。Engram 真正的护城河，可能不是训练管线的效率，而是他们对"哪条记忆属于哪个语境"的拓扑判断。

一个让我自己也意外的延伸预测

如果 Engram 成功，五年后我们会看到一个奇怪的劳动法新概念："神经离职协议"（neural exit agreement）。Dan 自己已经在访谈结尾暗示了——员工离职时，可以带走"被消毒过的技能权重"，但不能带走公司 IP 权重。这意味着 NDA 将从文本契约演化为权重契约：你签的不再是"不泄露 X"，而是"允许从你的 LoRA 中蒸馏出哪些子空间"。

这会催生一个全新的中介行业：权重审计师。律所和会计师事务所之外，第三类专业服务诞生——他们读的不是文档，而是激活模式。

📝 转录文本

开场：模型如何"神奇地"泛化

What about pre-training or even post-training makes it possible for the models to generalize in these magical emergent ways, and controlling that process so that a company has a set of private data? How do we make the models learn that just as well as the models know like the capital of France, or you know, like how to write Python? Um, so I think it's a really fun problem to think about.

[music]

欢迎来到 Training Data

Welcome to Training Data. We are delighted to have Dan Biderman and Jessy Lin, co-founders of Engram, today. Engram is a neo-lab focused on memory and continual learning — two of the hottest topics in all of AI research today. Okay. And Sean and I are delighted to dig in on those topics with you today.

Awesome. Happy to be here.

Great.

"我们的模型永远在训练"——Engram 的核心理念

So maybe to kick off, the Engram website says, "We don't see the world through the lens of pre-training or post-training. Our models are always training." What does that mean?

So I think like models today obviously know a lot of things. They're incredibly smart. Um, but we kind of think the bottleneck for making these models more useful these days is not really raw intelligence, but understanding like new and evolving context. So whether it's a new task that you're doing or a particular context, you know, like for a job or something like this — how do you bake that into the model weights the same way that pre-training and post-training bakes into the model weights very deeply? And this is kind of why we think of ourselves as working on these fundamental problems of memory and continual learning, which are really two sides of the same coin: how do you make the models learn new things and bake them deeply into the weights of the model?

外部记忆 vs 内部记忆

And is your premise then that memory as a separate database, or separate, you know, thing that you shove into the context window, is not true memory and it's not true continual learning?

I think all of these tools will kind of come together. So these days the way that people are solving these problems is with context engineering. So you take like a huge prompt, maybe you keep talking to the model over many many turns and hours, and reorganize the context to better understand what you're trying to do. And we think these kinds of things — like tool use, context engineering — will play a part. But I think an under-leveraged tool these days is using the same kind of training pipeline or framework or workflow that the frontier labs are using to make these models really good at frontier math or code, but applying that to every kind of domain, every kind of context that you have, like let's say in a company.

Yeah. And to me it's like — as an individual, taking notes and having sticky notes is a very valuable thing. We should never discard this. But whenever we get back to business the next day, we always have some sort of trace of memory in our brain, some new intuition about how things should be and where should we look. So these two things should come together. And current solutions are more kind of externalized memory. This has two issues. One is that the amount of tokens we will all collectively, individually generate is going to be in the tens of millions of tokens per day soon. So just keeping it and searching through it is going to be — and rereading it — it's going to be pretty expensive, but it's gonna also be pretty hard, pretty confusing for the models, unless we have major, major breakthroughs.

Billions of tokens for Sean. [laughter]

That's good. Depends on the day.

Engram 的产品形态

Could you maybe tell us a little bit about the Engram architecture or the Engram product and how it works?

Yeah. I mean, at a high level, I think what we're trying to do is take any context — like there's all these different workspaces, let's say. So we're working with partners like Notion and Microsoft and Harvey that have these places where people are doing a lot of work over a long period of time. There's all this context, both in terms of documents that you've already written as a team, as well as — now people are interacting with these agents more and more on these products, or having conversations, giving them feedback — and figuring out how to have a model that deeply understands that context. So not just reading the files at test time, but really understanding it the way that an employee that's worked at your company for years has. So you kind of understand at a high level: oh, these are the initiatives across the company, this is the way that we do things, you've studied like how to run the hiring pipeline or how to do this kind of thing within the company, and can operate just as well as anybody else can in the company.

And so what we're doing is training per-team models within these workspaces that deeply understand those contexts and can improve with time on the things that people care about. So the way that we do this at a technical level maybe is training these into weights. So we do a lot of adapter fine-tuning. So adapters of many types — like I think people have looked into this for decades at this point, whether it's LoRAs or prefixes or sparse architectures. I think all of these tools are at our disposal. And then figuring out what the right data is. So how do you turn any kind of raw document or interaction into useful training signal for the model? So again, we have a variety of tools now like supervised fine-tuning, RL, on-policy distillation — all these things that the field has kind of developed — and trying to fit these pieces together into a model that learns continuously on the things that people care about.

Yeah. And it's not a bet that tools are not there — like our models always work under the assumption that some knowledge is externalized, some tools are always there. But what you need to do, and that's the hard task, is what needs to be internalized and what can be externalized. And even for stuff that's externalized, many individuals and companies have their own bespoke tools and ways of doing things. Not everyone has the same bash CLI tools that the frontier models are training on, and how to get the models to better understand your bespoke setup, I think is its own interesting thing.

适配器、权重访问与权衡

And so is the premise then that my Notion agent will be a custom agent that is LoRA fine-tuned, or you know, it's some way with an adapter tuned, so that it's constantly learning on new content that's added into my Notion workspace. Is that the premise?

Yeah. And they're working with many models, and they're the early users of all the frontier models, and they're probably going to keep doing that.

Does this approach work on the frontier models — or the closed frontier models — or...

We need white — you know, we need white box access to the weights, right? So we can partner with companies that have closed-source weights and do this with them, but it's easiest for us to do it with open-source models. But any model that's a transformer model, we can do our thing to it.

And what's the trade-off then when people are comparing the before and after using you? Is it that they're no longer sending so much context, and so the trade-off is like you burn more compute up front to learn your company's way of doing things into the weights, and then you're sending less context to the model on every inference pass? Is that the rough trade-off?

That's one thing. The fact that you don't have to research things, and the fact that you don't have to reread things, and the fact that you don't have to write monstrous system prompts that already — that can give you two orders of magnitude reduction in token inference consumption. It's not like 50%, or it can be 100x fewer tokens, because many things — especially things that relate to people and teams and organization and priorities — these are things that you can't really find in one document, unless you really have it really regimented and document everything. And these kinds of things the model can kind of implicitly learn by training on some of the data and answer within 100 tokens what the best frontier models would consume 100,000 tokens doing. So these kinds of examples are interesting.

And also the quality — there are tasks that are not supernatural for the current generation of the models, and we kind of think there's going to be consistently this gap of like three to six months ahead, where there's certain things that are bespoke that people are just exploring, the models are not fully great for them. The models will at some point be great for them, but if you can autonomously learn in a very lightweight way, it will give value in that time in terms of capabilities.

为什么从团队层开始而不是个人

Why train on the workspace level versus the individual level, for example?

Either is fine for us. It's just easier to start with — you know, teams of people have are more disciplined in how they collect context, and in the amount of context they have over years, and it's easy for us to start there. But every person's computer and every person's phone, one day, is a useful target for our technologies, and in fact it will be very interesting to go there. We just think the big deposits of information are now in teams of people collaborating in knowledge work.

事实记忆：feature 还是 bug？

Is it a feature or a bug that there is so much fact memorization basically built into large language models? And there's a school of thought that, you know, the models just rote-memorizing the fact that the capital of France is Paris is actually a bad thing. And what we would prefer for the models to do is abstractly learn the concepts of countries and capital cities — but not to memorize all these facts in the weights. And so I'm curious what you think about disentangling memorization versus learning, how it's done in the models today, and then how you're thinking of approaching it.

Yeah, I think it's a really interesting question. Like, to some extent, you kind of need to remember stuff in order to compose them into more complex concepts. I think the thing that's kind of missing is figuring out what's important to remember. And I think even now when you think about learning new knowledge — if you look at a lot of these academic benchmarks, it's like, how can we learn very specific facts like the length of a bridge in this African country? And that's not something that you really want the models to devote capacity for, and it's not something that we devote capacity to.

So I think if you look at human memory — I mean, you can say a lot more about this — but it's lossy. Because part of the feature of intelligence is compressing what's important and separating that from what's not important. And so I think you can't really separate fact learning from non-fact learning or skill learning, as some people would like to think. Like if you take a model — and some people have done this with models where you strip out all the facts and just have it like the pure core or something like this — it's very unnatural as a model, it doesn't know basic things, and you kind of need that.

Why do you need that? Like, why can't you look up facts and then just have...

I think if you look at how the models think — if you need to recall basic facts in order to take the next step in your thinking, you can't get very far. Maybe that's like a high-level intuition, but it's part of the reason why we think training is really important. In order to think more and more complex and deep thoughts about things, you kind of need to internalize something so that you can compose them into more abstract concepts.

Yeah. And there have been efforts before that were hard to scale to try and disentangle the two and pre-train the models in a way that allows it to retrieve and search for things and not internalize them. It's just the recipe we know to hill-climb on collectively right now is this fact pre-training step. And I think the magic — or the mystery — of this approach is that traditionally in CS we would have databases as its own curriculum and we would have algorithms; and the databases is like facts about the world and capitals of whatever — store them, query them — and there's also algorithms of how do you efficiently manipulate information and get some answers in a sample-efficient way.

And I think the magic of deep learning is that these two things are now mushed together, and we need all these smart people in Anthropic interpretability to try and break them apart. And I think a lot of what we're seeing now in the adoption of AI into the economy is that these things are gradually separating again — where companies have their own context and they really handle them with care and engineer them with care, and there's a generic model that's completely a stranger to these contexts, and the model is operating on them. But for us, it's clear that there needs to be a certain convergence, at least with some cadence, where the facts and the stories and the details are getting mixed into the model. It has disadvantages as well, because, you know, capitals of countries — they can change, but it's not very frequent. But there's many other facts that are changing all the time, and just imprinting them into weights is a challenging thing to do.

I see. So you're saying it's a false dichotomy that's trying to separate algorithms from databases here. What really matters is how to distinguish what's important to remember versus what's not important.

Exactly.

来自做梦的灵感

And it's an open how we dream. Are you guys taking any inspiration from that in terms of ranking?

Very, very loosely. I think just the idea that that's kind of a phase that's missing maybe, where you take a context and you deeply internalize it. Right now it's like everything happens at test time — you look at the context that the user gives you and you do some thinking on the fly. But again, you can't get very far. Or you can get so far, maybe, and you make mistakes along the way. Like how do you digest that back into the model so that next time you do it, you do it the right way and make even more progress?

Yeah. And what are dreams? Dreams are pretty crazy things to say — we want to build an AI that's like our dreams sounds a little bit like a nut thing to do. There's not a lot of coherence there. But what's interesting there is — what happens in our dreams: we see things, we talk to ourselves, and we experiment with the affordances of what can we do and can't we do in the world, in social situations, and in any — it's heavily biased towards social stuff, right? So for us too with things we're building, you give the models the time to then go back, retreat from the actual interaction, and experiment with its affordances: what can it do in an environment, what does it know, how fast can it handle these tail extreme things — same ones that we dream about at night.

学术背景与一个典型场景

You guys come from academic backgrounds. Like, what's a canonical example that motivates this problem, or that's a win so far?

Yeah, I have one example, maybe Jessy can give another one. A hypothetical one: for example, imagine one of the AI labs, say OpenAI, has to win some math olympiad in a week's time from now. Would they construct a catalog of all the math textbooks and really have people annotate which chapters to get and which graphs to see? Or will they actually collect this, synthesize some training data, launch a training job, see where it lands in five-six days, start evaluating it, and stuff like that? So it's obvious for anyone who's trained models that there's a superior way to integrate across the ideas and capabilities, and it involves this kind of magic of training. And we are clear that this has to happen in those high-stakes domains of math and coding and cyber and stuff. We just think much of this magic can actually end up in the hands of many more people, and in interesting ways.

为什么不是前沿实验室自己做？

Like, why isn't it just the foundation model labs that own the end product here? How do you go between giants?

Yeah. So I think the worldview that we have is a bit different from the frontier lab worldview, where it's like: we want one model that's bigger and bigger, that's more and more intelligent across a variety of domains. Instead, how we see it — we kind of imagine this world where everybody has their own model. A lot of the things that people want to learn are either private — like things that will never see the light of day in a post-training data set — or even conflicting. Like, oh, the way that I want to do the task is different from how another company or another individual wants to.

And I think a lot of these things, we're already seeing, are hard to train into the models with the same tools that we have used for decades in machine learning, which is like you have really clean supervision, you have ground truth reward signals, and you create a nice environment and you train the model to use the tools to better accomplish this coding task. And instead, a lot of the things that actually happen out in the world are very ambiguous, or it's hard to say what makes something good. And so I think a lot of these things are very specific to individuals, and I think very kind of misaligned, or not very aligned, with how the frontier labs think about the whole training pipeline and what kind of models will exist in the longer term.

Yeah. And to add to it, I think — what is the P-zero for the frontier labs? And some of you here are pretty close with them. It's getting to AGI, getting this one generic model that's extremely capable in coding and math, and then using it to automate the economy or to solve really hard, long-term problems in cryptography and defense or whatever. And it's pretty clear what needs to happen to push this: more pre-training, bigger models, more data, more RL, more inference-time compute. That kind of stuff. That's P-zero. That's where the majority of expenditure and talent goes.

And definitely all of them are thinking about memory, and all of them are thinking about continual learning. It's just more of a product kind of effort right now. We think it deserves its own attention, and we think breakthroughs need to happen there. And Demis, at the Sequoia event about a month ago, said pretty clearly: we need new breakthroughs around these topics, and obviously they're thinking about them. We're just focusing exclusively on this, and we think certain things around incentives of where the data is and who owns the model are pretty interesting. So if you could learn from many humans or organizations at scale without necessarily sending someone to work with them shoulder-to-shoulder, that would be a pretty big unlock.

And maybe another point on that is — I think a lot of things need to look different in the world. So one is: there needs to be new research breakthroughs. Two: new infrastructure for training — like small models for everybody, rather than one big model, one big run. And then the third, I think, is a different way of combining research and product. So right now I think there's like researchers in these frontier labs — they kind of train the model, they throw it over the fence to the product team, who then prompts or context-engineers new product surfaces on top of the core models. But in this world where the models are always training, I think the inputs that users provide are very intricately tied to what the models learn from — like what the training signal is. And so there needs to be a lot more of an integrated loop between research and product. And so while we're focused on tackling a lot of the core research challenges, and that's our background, I think we're also very focused on how to deploy this as quickly as possible to learn from actual feedback in the real world.

动机：为什么是这个问题？

What motivated you to work on this problem?

I think it's obviously one of the grand challenges in AI. I think everybody's talking about it these days because the models are so smart — what else is left? It's learning at the edges, like learning the remainders of what makes these models useful. It's not just about raw intelligence anymore. It's about learning new things. And I think it also feels very fundamental, because it kind of goes back to really understanding what makes the model so good.

So right now the models kind of incidentally know a lot of things from pre-training, and we don't really understand why. It's like the internet was just this gift granted to us, where there's a diverse set of data that contains all of these different examples of coding and writing and all these other things, and it just happened that way. And now to figure out how to crack this problem of continual learning, it's about figuring out what about pre-training or even post-training makes it possible for the models to generalize in these magical emergent ways, and controlling that process — so that, you know, a company has a set of private data, how do we make the models learn that just as well as the models know the capital of France or how to write Python? So I think it's a really fun problem to think about.

Dan 的神经科学背景

And Dan, you came from the neuroscience world. Is that right?

Yes, yes. So I was initially interested in questions around consciousness and the human condition and things like that.

Are the models conscious?

I don't have any advanced thoughts on this more than you would read. I don't think so. But it's important that smart people are thinking about it. I would say I was interested in how humans think, how humans perceive. And as Amos Tversky, the Israeli psychologist, used to say — he's not interested in artificial intelligence, he's interested in natural stupidity.

So I would say I started kind of similarly, trying to see how people and animals experience the world. Gradually, my inclinations took me to the stats and AI domains, and there I figured that so many of the same problems of memory and continual learning are really, really urgent. And the kind of solutions we have in the current systems are pretty far from what we have in biology. And I'm not one of these people who would say that the machine should be like the animal or the human brain — I don't think so. There's many things computers can do better than us. But human memory has these very different things in it. You know, if you want to store a whole codebase, you can use a computer — you don't even need AI on the computer to store everything losslessly and just get it. But the human brain evolved to work in these constraints of information capacity, and to have these fuzzy representations that can then be abstracted and form connections and inform the next day. Current systems don't really have that beyond the generic pre-training step. And I was really interested in: what are ways to build that in, what are ways to learn from that.

哲学题：记忆是涌现性质吗？

This is more of a philosophical question. You mentioned in the brain how there's a bunch of different real estate — there's different co-processing units. Whatever modern computer architecture — there's CPUs, GPUs, memory, there's different co-processors. With like the bitter lesson, do you think that what's happening is that LLMs are converging to say one co-processor that's just totally dominant — it's like everything, all compute is going to happen in the GPU equivalent of a language model? Or do you think that these models are kind of building a bunch of co-processors emergently inside the model? Like, take memory: do you think that the models themselves will just build whatever part-of-the-brain equivalent would be that's good at memory, or do you think there needs to be like another standalone architecture?

Yeah, like, is memory an emergent property almost versus...? And almost everything — is everything that we need in intelligence will just be emergent with better training data and more scaled compute?

Yeah. I would say, just on a more superficial perspective on the current deployment of AI, it's way more than just GPUs, and we're seeing all these sandboxes exploding, and models operating on other computers, trying things...

I'm more, I mean, on the model architecture level rather than on the...

So other experiment — there have been many previous experiments on different architectures that we contributed to, like the state space family and others, to try and handle very, very long context more efficiently. The thing with all these methods, it ends up being a trade-off — usually a trade-off between memory and accuracy. And memory not in the behavioral, cognitive sense, memory in the computer sense, right? Instead of having the memory footprint of the transformer attention, which is quadratic in the sequence length, these models have...

Some are claiming, you know, they have sub-quadratic.

Yeah, some claim, and some do have it, right? And some of the best Chinese models have layers that are inspired by those state space architectures and are not quadratic in cost. The thing is, in our hands, we find that you always compromise accuracy for this memory. There's no free lunch. And what we're saying is — look, if you're really bitter-lesson-pilled, what you want to do is, you want to think: how can I burn more compute, and how can I burn it on new context that I have not seen before? So we're as bitter-lesson-pilled as anyone else, and we are not betting that the overall direction of AGI is going to end anywhere soon. We just think there's more compute to scale, and if I truly want to understand Sean's work and Sean's context, just rereading files is not going to make it — especially for a special person like you.

We got too derogatory.

We got to train [laughter] 100 trillion parameter for this guy.

Cosine, cosine.

用户最关心模型学到什么？

Um, what are you finding that people care most about their models learning? Is it memorizing facts about the organization? Is it remembering "ah, no, we do CI this way"? What are people actually hoping for? And then maybe this feeds into how you do the ranking of memory slots and all that?

Yeah. Well, I think if you look at what people are spending their time in the app layer doing these days, it's a lot of just trying to make the model work well for your use case. Like, "oh, I want the model to design my website with my brand style." That's a very common example these days. But there's many kinds of different tasks that people do with agents. Like learning how to run a workflow, or your particular way of writing, let's say. So there's many many kinds of things. And honestly, I think when we think about these methods — kind of going back to this distinction between facts and skills — there really is none. I think the methods are kind of agnostic to that.

Yeah. To me, it's like the natural thing — almost all the app layers are basically a frontier model wrapped in a loop with search tools and stuff. And what they're all interested in doing with us is finding ways to interface with their data in a way that's faster, more efficient, and also is more contextual. So almost all of them — it's like, we want to have our firm knowledge be encoded in something that's more efficient that I don't have to research. We want to have the model know in a targeted way who's the person I should triage a thing to. And we're just showing them that with pretty lightweight training, these things can be instinctual to the models. They don't have to have these very involved long ripple loops to solve them. So it's in a sense it's like a RAG killer kind of thing. Again, we can always do RAG and we can always retrieve, but that's the thing that people are interested in: interfacing with very large data planes and automating very repetitive things this way.

RAG 杀手？深入辨析

Yeah. And I want to double-click on this RAG killer thing — and I'm sorry to beat a dead horse, I just don't fully grok it yet. Is the premise that there's some trade-off between doing RAG versus updating your model weights? Is it the idea that you should be doing both? Like, what types of things should be done in the weights versus what types of things should be externalized to RAG?

I think it's an unsolved problem. I don't think anyone has answered it. We're all working on it. It's also the fundamental question of biological memory: what should be internalized versus what not. I do think that things that are like — do you need to internalize the room number in a hotel that you were in like a year ago? Probably no, not in your neural tissue. Probably that's good to write down. But do you need to internalize maybe the password to your home right now? Probably it's useful for the next few years to have that imprinted somewhere. So yeah, how does this translate into knowledge work and products? This is still something we figure out. And we try to take the approach that we try to use as few heuristics as possible. It's easy to run filters on the data and say, "I'm going to keep this, discard that, train on this, train on that." But as humans, we watch TikTok and we get exposed to a lot of garbage, and still the brain is able to learn and not completely go off the rails. And we think models should be the same as well.

Yeah. Maybe concretely in the short term, I think a lot of what people are worried about these days is the huge inference costs of running these agents like for days on end.

High inference costs — a good thing. [laughter]

I mean, consuming tokens for what?

Sonya works with Fireworks. She really loves [laughter] inference.

We love inference too.

Yeah. So I think it's like in the short term, that's the immediate pain point. Like, why are you reading the same files over and over again, you know, even in the same query? But definitely, across people in the same company, they're running the same queries on the same documents over and over again, and that should be something the model just knows. Like in the same way you ask an employee, they don't type into the search box "what was I working on yesterday?" — they just know.

But doesn't caching kind of solve that?

I think to some extent, yeah. But I think going back to this question of what should be internalized versus what's something you retrieve at test time — I think again, a lot of it is about building on your knowledge. So if you are always doing RAG, you can't make associations like: oh, I see somebody on the team is doing this kind of research, and I kind of recall at an abstract level — oh, there's this related thing that you might want to know about. You didn't even ask about it, right? But I think these kinds of associations can only happen in weights, because they're not really about "you ask me to search for this, I'm going to search for this."

Yeah. And also, the main limitation with retrieval systems in general and in AI specifically is like — the problem is not so much what to store and where to put it. The problem is, how to address it, like how to query the thing. Do you know what to look for, even?

And this involves some sort of intuition that sometimes the models don't have. Interestingly enough, they don't know where to look. And especially if you're limited to the current way of doing things, which is keyword search that is just easier to scale in RL and less involved in terms of infra for embeddings and stuff. So yeah, knowing what to search is something that's intuitive and can happen in the weights.

KV Cache：一个怪物

And also about caching and inference — like, much of this company started with us taking a deep dive into KV caches and caching. And this is a fascinating thing, right? KV cache is a monstrosity of the current way of doing things. Think about it: a KV cache for a single Wikipedia article for some Taylor Swift or something like this — it will be like 80 gigabytes of HBM memory on the GPU. And an entire — it's for, say, a 70B Llama model — and the entire weights of the model would be about 100 gigabytes, and with some distortion, they remember the entire internet. And how come this thing is so bit-efficient?

One thing is, we have this proof of existence that gradient descent can pack a lot of information in very few numbers, whereas this KV cache thing — you take a few tens of kilobytes of article and it becomes those 80 gigabytes of brain state. So sure, you can cache this, you can load this, you'll have issues with disk to HBM stuff — people are working on it. It's pretty interesting. But what if we can take those 80 gigabytes, spend some compute offline (maybe also in Fireworks and file), but then compress it and make it really really small, so that the thing we load in cache is like a 1000x smaller? That would have tremendous implications for how we load things, how fast we can do things, and what the fidelity of the representation is.

Super interesting.

"记忆的 ChatGPT 时刻"会是什么样？

Yeah. What are some of the things that could happen in the next year or two that would be like the ChatGPT moment of memory? Or do you think that that's not how things will play out?

It's a good question. I don't know. I think like the first proof of concept of the thing that people keep talking about with continual learning, which is: you have an intern that you can teach things over time and it actually gets better. I think everybody's waiting to see that. And no matter how sophisticated the context engineering approaches are these days, they're not getting there. So I think you need all of these tools at your disposal to make that happen. But I think it will be something like that, where it's like, the model's actually getting smarter — like, whoa, it's different from yesterday.

Yeah. And it's important to say that the ChatGPT model was not anticipated. We've just read about all the different product directions that certain people had before ChatGPT was — different. To me, the example is: look, if you resigned from your job today and your sole mission was to make a model that's better for you, and you would use OpenAI and Anthropic and all these frontier models, and you just 24/7 engineer the context, write skills — your way to move the needle is very limited as an individual. You'll just be better off waiting for the next version of the model and you'll take it from there. And we would like to see a future where actually the more time you spend on the thing actually translates to the quality of performance, at least in the things and domains you care about. And this is pretty hard to achieve, and the only reason it could be achieved is if you start scaling compute and training on these data without destroying them all — importantly — which is pretty hard.

快问快答：最近一次让你惊讶的 AI 进展

Just a couple — like, this is just for fun — like rapid-fire questions going off just memory. When's the last time you were surprised about something in AI, in any area?

When reading about fundraising? [laughter]

A lot of surprises every day. I would say all of us felt a little bit of a change around the capabilities of the coding agents. That's true. But we've been dabbling with these things and trying to make them work in more effortful ways before, so it didn't come as a complete surprise. But yeah, I think to me the main events were GitHub Copilot — that for me was just the main event — and ChatGPT, and then seeing the agentic stuff. We all anticipated, I think, and different people had different expectations on how far it can go and how long horizon it can go. But I feel — yeah, we're yet to see something fundamentally different, and people are working on completely new ways of doing things now. But yeah, to me, it's models actually changing in a way that's not harmful and learning new things on the fly that are personally and economically viable. That's interesting.

记忆钱包：未来形态？

Right now there's this idea of like, we're each going to have a token wallet that we're going to bring around to companies or to different apps, different workspaces. Do you think that we're gonna end up with like a memory bank, a memory wallet that we're going to move around across the digital world as we go?

I think it's an interesting question. I don't know if we've fully figured out what the right product form factor is in this sense. In a way, even with ChatGPT memory, let's say, I kind of don't want it to remember across my personal and work context. Yeah. Like, it's like, "oh, you might like these sheets because you trained a model on a GPU last week" — it's like, that's totally irrelevant. And to some extent, it's like because the memory is flawed. But also, I think you do want memory in your tools and the products that you use to be separated, to have control over that. So I personally think there needs to be some separation there, but I guess, to be determined what that might look like.

Yeah. And like, I think a holy grail is — you go to work and you just burn through all these tokens and you create all this value, and somehow all the IP and stuff stays with the company, but somehow the skills you learned, the things you invented, your ways of doing things — some of them you can take with you as well to your next job in a way that's sanitized and not harmful to any other company's IP. So I do think carrying a set of skills will be interesting. We do it in our biology right now, and we just sign NDAs and have ethical rules around it. But I think doing it in a digital world would be pretty interesting and pretty rewarding, because it will force each of us to push the frontier and implement AI more deeply in our companies, in our individual life, and then be rewarded for it.

视觉 vs 语言：一个"民科理论"

I started a PhD in the stats firm in 2007 at Stanford, and AI like AI was boring as hell at the time. It was all statistical learning, and there's basically two areas: computer vision and NLP. So like, vision and language were kind of the two areas. And I think that's still true. In 2012, AlexNet happened — vision was dominating for six years or whatever. Are you guys surprised that language seems to be like the language approach seems to be dominating over vision in progress? Question two: do you think vision has any chance of coming back? How do you think about this?

Yeah, I think it is pretty surprising to me. I mean, some people maybe saw it coming, but I think I've always kind of been interested in language as, I don't know, I guess like a medium for communication, and so many kind of complex abstract things can be done in language. I do think like, in the longer term, language and vision will kind of combine in this more unified system where we kind of take in inputs from all of these different modalities and understand them in this abstract way. But, um, yeah.

Yeah. To me, like, I've never been interested in language. It seemed to me such an advanced capability that — the entire animal kingdom has very different forms of speech and language than what we, you know, and how we communicate with ourselves and in writing. And I was always, as many other leaders in AI had this thought, that the natural thing is — you have to experience the world, act in it, and vision and action — that will be the key.

But then I've, like anyone else, seen the ChatGPT moment, and went to do some work at Mosaic and stuff like that to learn how the sausage is made on the NLP side. And the thing that's striking is that the language should be pretty hard. Like, each word has this one-hot embedding vector that's as dissimilar to any other word as it is, you know, it's a completely high-dimensional space and it's really artificial in a sense. And we learn it with models that are an order of magnitude bigger than the best vision models, and still things work pretty well. I do think there's a lot of juice to be squeezed in an image and video, and I think you guys do good investments in this space, but I think the two will keep being interesting in different ways.

主持人的"民科假说"

I'm now going to tell you — that was my lead-up. Now I'm going to tell you the crackpot theory. [laughter] And this podcast is not for me to pontificate. It's for you guys. But this is something I've been thinking a lot about, and you're the right people to share this with.

I was pretty shocked that language kind of surpassed vision, and I underestimated what was happening with LLMs in like 2018, 2019, 2020 because I just had this bias towards vision. And when I look back on it now, I think what is basically happening is that in biology, vision has a massive fundamental advantage over language in biology. And maybe I'm wrong, but basically, the bit rate that your brain can process optical data through the eye is — and this is my, I'm not a biologist, this is just kind of my dumb assessment — seems many orders of magnitude greater. And there's a lot of optical processing that happens even before you reach electrons. And so just the total bit rate of training data that's being processed and then making it to your brain seems many orders of magnitude greater than the audio data, where, you know, it's sound waves — where sound waves are fundamentally much slower bit rate than light.

And then there's almost like an upscaling from the acoustics to electronics which make it into your brain, where it's — there's like a downscaling from photons to electrons with vision. Whereas in computers today, everything is electronic. So it's kind of like you nerfed vision and you promoted language, where all processing is on the same playing field — it's all electronic.

And I just — this is like my crazy-ass, dumb, non-technical crackpot theory — but I think this might be part of why, just from an information theory perspective, that maybe language and vision are on a similar playing field by the time you get to LLMs. And then LLMs are — we're just a really really smart architecture that's better suited for language than for vision. How dumb does this sound, especially to you Dan, the neuroscientist?

Jessy also has some background in cognitive computational science, right?

So I would say my point here is — look, much of what we're doing in knowledge work, we haven't evolved to do, right? We're sitting on these computers reading these things, writing these memos, whatever. We are not evolved to do this. Our brains are not wired for this. Still, nevertheless, it's useful to have LLMs to do this for us. And as humans, we're heavily vision-biased. Other rodents are more olfactory-biased, and I've worked on these things myself before. So what's the real estate in the brain that's allocated to vision (the occipital lobes), versus language areas (the temporal lobe)? Probably more vision. I'll have to check with — check ChatGPT — but I think that's the situation.

You don't know from memory.

No, man, I'm externalizing. I'm a big RAG believer in my personal lifestyle. [laughter]

In the limit, we're all — it's all RAG. I internalize just, you know, important things — like my emotions to you. No, just kidding. Sorry. [laughter]

Anyway, and vision is dominating when people are training vision-language models — they end up the language ends up dominating the vision content there. But yeah, it's hard to say that because a certain brain is more biased towards a certain modality doesn't mean necessarily that we're going to more efficiently do it. I do think that efforts on brain-computer interfaces should take this into account: how do you then relay it back to the brain? That's where I think it's really important to think — what real estate do we have there right now? But for knowledge work, it's equally fine if it's text, I think.

收官：五到十年后的世界

Last question: if everything goes right, what does the world look like in five to ten years, and then what is Engram's role in it?

I think I'm imagining a world where everyone has their own model that is really different from the other person's model and from the frontier model, and all of these kind of serve different purposes. And to have a model that really — you know, I think people often talk about knowing you, but also kind of helping you in the ways that make sense to you personally, whether it's an individual or a team. I think there's an element of having different kinds of intelligence everywhere.

Yeah. And to me, actually, it's a variant of the story where — in neuroscience, we know that memory and navigation are pretty closely related. Same circuits in the brain that represent landmarks in space are in charge of some elements of episodic memory and things like this. And for me, I think the company can be the actual LLM interface to the data plane for everyone. So sharing some similarities to great companies like Databricks and Oracle, where — we form these memories that happen to be neural memories, with models that happen to be personalized, and happens to be there's hundreds of millions of them. But they're basically a neural interface to the data plane in a way that's very different from what we know, and it's more efficient, it's more associative. It's not representing the file system as it is — it's representing a brain state of that file system. So that's for me a vision.

Beautiful vision to end on. Thank you guys so much for coming by and sharing what you're building.

Awesome. Love it. Thank you guys.

[music]

观看原视频 →
item.description:
## 一、最反直觉的观察：KV Cache 是"硅基大脑的胎记"
Dan 在访谈中埋了一个极具张力的数字对比，但他自己没有把它推到最深处——让我替他完成这次顿悟：
一篇 Taylor Swift 的维基百科条目（几十 KB 文本），在 70B Llama 的 KV cache 里要膨胀成 **约 80 GB 的 HBM 状态**；而整个 70B 模型的权重也才约 100 GB，里面"压缩"了几乎整个互联网。
这意味着：**模型的"长期记忆"压缩比是其"工作记忆"压缩比的 10⁶ 量级。** 换句话说，今天的 LLM 是一个有着博士级长期记忆、却用整面墙的便利贴来记今天对话的怪物。所谓"上下文工程"，本质是在用胶带把这个不对称结构补起来。Engram 真正在做的，不是"加一层记忆"，而是**修复硅基智能与生俱来的代谢失衡**——把短期昂贵的 KV 状态，逐步代谢成长期廉价的权重状态。
这恰好对应生物学中的 **"记忆固化"（memory consolidation）**：海马体中昂贵、高保真、易丢失的短时记忆，在睡眠期间被慢慢翻译成皮层中廉价、抽象、持久的长时记忆。Engram

item.title: [Mercatus Center] Bobbi Herzberg — 2025 Markets and Society Conference Keynote
item.dc:creator: Mercatus Center
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

三个反直觉的洞察

一、奥斯特罗姆最激进的命题，是"秩序根本不来自秩序"

表面上他在讲分权、联邦制、多中心治理——这些都太"政治学教科书"了。真正颠覆的是他在底层下的赌注：秩序不是被设计出来的，而是从共同信念中"凝结"出来的。这意味着宪法、法律、官僚体系在因果链上其实是结果，不是原因。我们习惯把制度当成自变量，把行为当成因变量；奥斯特罗姆把这个方向反了过来。这也解释了一个让人不安的推论：任何试图"通过设计制度来制造好公民"的努力，因果方向是颠倒的，注定低效甚至适得其反。这与当代许多"用算法治理人"的硅谷思路恰好相反。

二、一个让人意外的观察："国会不是审议机构"——因为人不能同时说和听

Bobby 转述 Vincent 时埋了一颗炸弹：众议院太大，所以根本无法"审议"。这听起来像政治学评论，但它其实是一个信息论命题——基于"一次只有一个人能说话"这种近乎生理学的约束。换言之，民主的尺度上限不是由宪法决定的，而是由人类听觉带宽决定的。这把民主理论一下子拉到了认知科学的层面：

  • 与香农的信道容量定理相通：群体的共识带宽 = 串行对话速率 × 互相理解的耐心；
  • 与邓巴数（150 人社交上限）共振：超过这个尺度，"治理"必然滑向"统治"；
  • 与现代社交媒体形成讽刺反差：算法让"所有人同时对所有人说话"，于是看似放大了民主，实际上摧毁了民主赖以存在的串行对话结构。

如果这条诊断成立，那么"民主的危机"就不是右翼或左翼的问题，而是尺度错配问题——我们用对话生物适配的工具，去运行 3.3 亿人的系统。

三、跨领域连接：民主治理 = 爵士乐 + 生态系统 + 工匠学徒制

奥斯特罗姆的"多中心治理"在 Bobby 的描述里同时援引了三个完全不同领域的隐喻： - 生态学——森林里没有一棵"主树"，物种通过互动维持韧性； - 音乐——交响乐 vs 爵士乐：一个强加秩序，一个在协作中发现秩序； - 工匠传统——公民通过"做"学会自治，就像艺人通过手工学会技艺。

把这三者叠加起来会得到一个反传统经济学的洞察：自治不是一种"制度"，而是一种"手艺"。手艺无法被规模化生产，无法被外包，无法被自动化——它只能被亲手传承。这就解释了为什么 Vincent 坚持"学者必须下场参与政策"——你不能教别人骑车而自己从未骑过。

真正惊人的推论：如果民主是手艺，那么民主的真正威胁不是独裁者，而是便利。每一次"让专家替我决定"、"让 AI 帮我投票"、"让算法过滤我的信息"，都在让这门手艺退化一点点。Vincent 担心的"Faustian bargain"在 2026 年的今天有了新的面孔——它不再叫"中央集权"，而叫"用户体验"。

最后一个埋伏笔：注意 Bobby 选了一张 Vincent 在曼尼图林岛、连电都没有的照片来谈"自治的脆弱"。这不是怀旧——这是一个断网隐喻。在没有外部基础设施可以依赖的地方，人才不得不重新成为"自己的政府"。换句话说，自治能力与外部基础设施的丰裕度，可能成反比。这对一个被云服务、智能家居、AI 助手包围的时代，是一个极冷的判断。

📝 转录文本

主持人开场

You're listening to the Hayek Program Podcast. This podcast includes audio from lectures, interviews, and discussions from scholars and visitors of the F. A. Hayek Program for Advanced Study in Philosophy, Politics, and Economics at the Mercatus Center at George Mason University. To learn more about the Hayek Program, visit hayek.mercatus.org. To learn about graduate student fellowship opportunities with the Mercatus Center at George Mason University — for students at Mason as well as at universities across the globe — please visit students.mercatus.org.

嘉宾介绍

Bobbi Herzberg is a distinguished senior fellow at the F. A. Hayek Program for Advanced Study in Philosophy, Politics, and Economics at the Mercatus Center at George Mason University, and an instructor emeritus at Utah State University. She's a past president of the Public Choice Society and the Society for the Development of Austrian Economics.

Bobbi has extensive experience in academic administration, having served as the department head of the political science department and administrative director of the Institute for Political Economy at Utah State, and extensive experience in philanthropy — having worked at the John Templeton Foundation and serving on boards of various organizations.

Her scholarship focuses on institutional economics, public choice, the Bloomington School (having taught there along with the Ostroms), and public policy. Specifically, she explores health care policy, the potential of self-governance to provide health care and welfare programs, environmental policy, and much more.

Bobbi upholds the importance of theory and practice and the multifaceted, interdisciplinary way that we need to study the world around us. Institutional analysis and governance matter for how we live. She thoughtfully balances the need for grounded scholarship and practical policy solutions, and she pushes her colleagues and students to do so too.

Tonight she will discuss Vincent Ostrom and the meaning of democracy. Please join me in welcoming Bobbi Herzberg.

开场寒暄

Well, it's a big crowd — and a big crowd of friends is sometimes the hardest crowd to talk to. Even though I'm sure you're all going to be friendly, um, because I care more what you think than a crowd of strangers. So, um, you be patient. Be patient with me.

I have the honor of talking about Vincent Ostrom and his theories of democracy with you tonight, and that is a real honor for me to be able to do that. But it's also not your best subject matter for a post-travel day, pre-bar experience. I know that this keynote should last no longer than 20 minutes to be really good, maybe 15 to be super good — and doing Vincent in that time...

So we're going to take the 30,000-foot vision of Vincent. The Markets and Society journal will publish a longer and more citation-filled version of this talk. But hopefully I can share at least some of the key ideas I think we should all appreciate in Vincent's work. And I know so many of you know so much about this work in any case that you'll go, "Well, why'd you leave that out? Why'd you leave that out?" Well, we'll discuss it over the next few days, I hope.

So it is a privilege. And the biggest — as I was starting to put this together, one of the things that I really appreciated was how much Lin Ostrom would have loved that we're doing this talk. She got much more tickled when somebody honored Vincent than even one of the great honors of her many honors that she had. So I'm thinking of her, and how she's thinking tonight about all of this and appreciating it.

So with that, I'm hoping I'm catching you at the right time — between just enough wine that you're pretty mellow, but not quite falling asleep yet. So hold with me. Okay, so let's go.

引入：民主作为人类的艺术

Tonight I'd like to talk about someone who spent his life helping us think more clearly about one of the most important and most difficult ideas in human history — the idea of democracy and how we sustain it.

Vincent Ostrom was not only a scholar but a craftsman of ideas — someone who believed that democratic life is a form of human artistry, something we make together and must constantly tend.

Vincent 的成长背景

Born in 1919 — love that — in Washington State, just close to the Canadian border in a very rural area, he grew up surrounded by forests, rivers, and farms. His father raised mink and bred mink. That early exposure to how people cooperated to manage shared resources — or didn't cooperate to manage those resources — stayed with him all of his life.

After teaching at the University of Wyoming and Oregon and then UCLA, he found his intellectual home at Indiana University, where he and Elinor Ostrom founded the Workshop in Political Theory and Policy Analysis. Together they built not just a research center but a living laboratory of self-governance. And why that is so important — that they were able to do that at Indiana University. And I know we have some people from Indiana University here tonight. It was such a special place for them to do that.

And it was important because at Wyoming and at UCLA, Vincent Ostrom had faced critics that said he should not be participating in the actual policy issues and policy design — it was inappropriate for a scholar. But that could not have been further from the truth for Vincent. He believed he had to be in the mix, because that was the most important goal of society. I miss them.

Vincent's great project was to understand how people can govern themselves without relying entirely on distant authorities or bureaucratic hierarchies. And that's where we begin tonight.

I have four key points — a fairly straightforward part of his theories on democracy, but profound, I think, insights that shaped his understanding that I'd like to share with you.

一张特别的照片

Now, this is my favorite Vincent picture, and it's my favorite Vincent picture because that little guy there is my 37-year-old son at an earlier time. You might imagine he's not that guy — whatever that movie was about the guy going backwards, I forget that one.

Um, but what I love about it — you may love it too — is Lin Ostrom brought back matching jackets from Africa for a toddler and Vincent, and they wore them. Looks good on Glenn; I'm not so sure it quite was Vincent's jam, but he wore it for that picture. So this is one of my very special favorite pictures. So I share that as my key points. One has nothing to do with the key points, but what the hell, it's my talk anyway.

第一点：秩序自下而上构成

Okay, so let's get back to those. So these are the key points, and I'm going to go through them in order, and hopefully give you just a highlight of each of them. So let's go ahead and get started.

Vincent began from a very simple but radical observation: order is not imposed from above. It is constituted from below. It emerges from our common beliefs.

He wrote, "Order in human societies is constituted by concepts and beliefs that human beings hold in common with one another as customs, laws, and rules."

That might sound abstract, but think about what it means. Every time you stop at a red light, return a library book, or don't grab your neighbor's cheesecake off the table, you are participating in a web of shared understanding about appropriate behavior. And that's the way we live most of our lives. No one forces you to do most of these things — at least past the age of your mother having any control over you — but you do them because you and those around you share certain expectations about what it means to live together.

Ostrom insisted that legitimacy in government rests not on command, but on consent and communication. As he put it, officials can command, but individuals do not necessarily obey. We obey when we believe that the rules make sense — when they resonate with our shared understanding of what's right. And so those are the rules that stick, and those are the rules that Vincent wanted to understand and wanted people to create for themselves.

与托克维尔的呼应

This is where Vincent's thinking overlaps very much with Alexis de Tocqueville. Both believed that religion, local custom, and community life shape how we interpret the actions of others and build trust. Shared knowledge doesn't just come from ideas, but from deeds — from the way we live together day after day.

对话的尺度限制

Ostrom also saw limits to this process. There's only so much that we can understand in large impersonal settings. Individuals engaged in discussion cannot speak and listen simultaneously. Although, goodness knows, I try to do so a lot. And probably some of you are trying to listen while looking at your phone and having conversations. I see you texting. This happens a lot at Mercatus. I'm just telling you right now — it's like their jam. Anyway, but he reminded us, you cannot do that. So earlier times — what can we say? You're all better than he was, I guess. Anyway.

That simple fact — one person talking at a time — places limits on how large a community of understanding can be. And boy, did he believe that. I can't tell you how many times we had the conversation, and he reminded me that the House of Representatives was not a deliberative legislative body. And this was very disturbing to a 27-year-old Congress scholar just starting her career. So, anyway — they can't deliberate because they're just too big. So that was his attitude on it. He thought that people needed to really get into the process to make the decisions that they reached.

And maybe that's one of the reasons why democracy seems so strained today. The scale of our political life has grown far beyond the scale of our conversations. We're trying to deliberate as one giant community of 330 million people — very diverse people — often through screens that make genuine listening almost impossible.

第二点：个人有自治的能力

Second key point that Vincent raises: individuals — I love this picture, too. It's a good one. This is Vincent in Alaska, because he made a difference in Alaska.

Individuals are very capable of self-government, from the perspective of Vincent Ostrom. He saw them governing. He saw them doing really hard things like constitution-making, and he participated in that. And as a result them working through these things like the Alaska Constitution on Natural Resources, Article 8 — which, you know, is part of the reason that people get a check now in the state of Alaska. Citizens all get royalties. Imagine your government just sending you money because it's your property, not theirs. Doesn't happen in all the other states, but it happens in Alaska. And he was at that conversation, because it came from the people — not from Vincent Ostrom, not from the expert.

Also, California irrigation water — really challenging circumstance. These are situations where we see how people working out the details listen to one another, argue with one another, have real challenges, but work to find common ground.

制度即手艺

He understood institutions not as static rules of the game, but as artifacts — things people craft using their local knowledge and skills. And just as artisans learn through practice, citizens learn to govern themselves by doing.

He once described a self-governing society as one that turns on the universality of artisanship. In such a society, each person first becomes their own governor — someone capable of self-mastery — and only then joins others to work out common arrangements.

This is a deeply hopeful vision of democracy. It's not about designing the perfect constitution or electing the right leaders. It's about cultivating the habits and virtues of self-governance — what Tocqueville called "the art of association."

But Ostrom was no romantic. He knew self-governance is hard work. People disagree. They misunderstand each other. They bring different histories and expectations to the table. That's why he valued institutions that slow things down — deliberative bodies, checks and balances, local councils — places where people can argue, reflect, and eventually find common ground. He honestly thought people could do that. I'm not so sure, but he honestly thought they could.

第三点：多中心治理 (Polycentricity)

And that's point three. Then how?

The concept most commonly associated with Vincent Ostrom and his theories of democracy is the theory of polycentricity, and polycentricity as good governance.

Together with Charles Tiebout and Robert Warren, he defined a polycentric system as one with many centers of decision-making that are formally independent of each other. In such a system, different problems can be addressed at different levels — some locally, some regionally, some nationally — without everything being run from the top down.

生态系统的类比

If we imagine different settings — such as maybe a forest ecosystem — there isn't one tree in charge. Instead, you have multiple species, each interacting and adapting to its environment. The result is a resilient whole.

That's what Ostrom wanted for human governance: a system of overlapping jurisdictions where cooperation, competition, and even conflict can coexist productively.

Okay, I have to make sure I'm on the right slide.

He wrote in The Crisis of Public Administration that centralization and decentralization must exist simultaneously. True federalism, in his view, means accepting the messiness of overlapping authority. We like things to be neat, but neat doesn't really work in large societies with subjective preferences and all kinds of things tearing people in different directions. It's most efficient in the bureaucratic sense, but it's far more adaptable and humane to work in this polycentric system.

对公民能力的要求

And that brings us to an important point. Polycentric systems rely on citizens who can navigate complexity. They demand judgment, tolerance, and engagement. If we expect Washington — or any central authority — to fix all our problems, we lose the vitality of democratic life. And we lose it to people who seem increasingly incapable of taking it up and taking up that task. And I'm not ready for that.

So polycentric governance isn't chaos. It's freedom structured through relationships of trust and accountability. It's the difference between a symphony — and a single symphony conductor — and a jazz ensemble. And I think I stole that from someone in this room. So if I did, just tell me who I did, so I'll cite you in the right thing, because I don't remember. But I thought that was really good with the jazz. Anyway — the first imposes order; the second discovers it through collaboration.

第四点：自治的脆弱性

The fourth key point — it's hard. The fragility of self-governance.

This is not an easy task, and they understood this. Both the Ostroms understood this. This is them on a hay wagon up — and they used to spend a lot of time up in Canada at Manitoulin Island and in weird places. But anyway, they did. Well, it was a weird place. No electricity, no anything. Thirty miles back and forth on bad roads once a week to get the mail. Imagine living as an academic — multiple academics, very popular academics — for the entire summer under those conditions. You guys couldn't leave your phones away for five minutes, you know. Anyway — oh, anyway, that's going to show up on the tape. Okay.

Faustian Bargain

So, it's fragile. Of course, Vincent also warned us that self-governance is fragile. It can be derailed by impatience, by the temptation to let others decide for us, or by the belief that unity requires uniformity.

He called this the Faustian bargain of politics. To create order, we need inequality of decision-making power — leaders, judges, administrators. But those same powers can easily become instruments of domination. "The sanctions necessary for maintaining order," he wrote, "may be used to compound wrongs as well as rights."

对中央集权的警告

Ostrom saw the dangers long before they became headlines: the tyranny of the majority, democratic despotism, the collapse of the rule of law. He warned that centralization in the presence of great diversity will inevitably entrap mankind in Catch-22 situations. In other words, when we try to solve every problem from the top, we create systems so rigid that they defeat their own purpose.

"Democracies," he wrote, "are vulnerable to the vices of rulers and the ineptitude of the people, and great experiments become monumental disasters."

Those are sobering words, but they're also an invitation to remember that democracy is not something we have — it's something we do. It's a practice, a set of relationships that must be renewed in every generation.

结语：民主是一场对话

So where does that leave us?

For Vincent, democracy was not a machine to be engineered, but a conversation to be sustained. It requires us to speak honestly, listen carefully, and act responsibly within the limits of our shared understanding.

We cannot all agree on everything — probably not even in this room, and we're kind of part of the same crowd — nor should we try. I mean, how boring would that be? I'm bored already thinking about it. Must be time for a drink, so I'll wrap up.

What we can do is build institutions, large and small, that let us live with our differences while still working toward common purposes. In that sense, the real measure of democracy for Vincent was not how efficiently we govern, but how generously we live together. He was very generous. He really was.

Vincent often thanked his students and colleagues for joining him in what he called "the adventure of self-governing." Tonight, I think he would thank all of us for keeping that adventure alive.

So let us raise a glass. Another great picture. This looks like he doesn't even know how to drink a beer, right? Doesn't it? I don't know. I mean, you know, he really wasn't of this world. He was kind of a different character altogether. I really loved — I mean, I loved him.

Um, so let us raise a glass, if you still have anything in it, to Vincent Ostrom — and to the ongoing experiment in freedom that he helped us understand.

Thank you.

节目结束语

Thank you for listening to the Hayek Program Podcast. To learn more about the research, scholars, and work of the Hayek Program, visit hayek.mercatus.org. For more information about graduate student fellowship opportunities for students at Mason as well as at universities across the globe, please visit students.mercatus.org. We hope you recommend students to our programs or consider applying yourself.

观看原视频 →
item.description:
## 三个反直觉的洞察
### 一、奥斯特罗姆最激进的命题，是"秩序根本不来自秩序" 表面上他在讲分权、联邦制、多中心治理——这些都太"政治学教科书"了。真正颠覆的是他在底层下的赌注：**秩序不是被设计出来的，而是从共同信念中"凝结"出来的**。这意味着宪法、法律、官僚体系在因果链上其实是**结果**，不是**原因**。我们习惯把制度当成自变量，把行为当成因变量；奥斯特罗姆把这个方向反了过来。这也解释了一个让人不安的推论：**任何试图"通过设计制度来制造好公民"的努力，因果方向是颠倒的，注定低效甚至适得其反**。这与当代许多"用算法治理人"的硅谷思路恰好相反。
### 二、一个让人意外的观察：**"国会不是审议机构"——因为人不能同时说和听** Bobby 转述 Vincent 时埋了一颗炸弹：众议院太大，所以根本无法"审议"。这听起来像政治学评论，但它其实是一个**信息论命题**——基于"一次只有一个人能说话"这种近乎生理学的约束。换言之，民主的尺度上限不是由宪法决定的，而是由**人类听觉带宽**决定的。这把民主理论一下子拉到了认知科学的层面：
- 与香农的信道容量定理相通：群

item.title: [投资TALK君] ✨【投资TALK君1444期】散户融资体量新高！芯片估值打满！这个标的加入观察列表 $CBRS✨20260623#CPI #nvda #美股 #投资 #英伟达 #ai #特斯拉
item.dc:creator: 投资TALK君
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、被忽略的"估值悖论"：芯片打满，而大科技反而被严重低估

小涛在视频中提到一个看似矛盾的现象——SOX芯片估值已经接近30倍上区间（打满），但纳指过去8个月只上涨7%，且这7%几乎全部由芯片贡献。这意味着除芯片外的"其他大科技"实际上处于隐性熊市中。

这里有一个让人意外的观察：市场表面上在"AI狂欢"，但资金其实只在AI产业链的最底层（硬件/芯片）狂欢，而上层应用、软件、平台型科技却在悄悄塌缩估值。这是一种典型的"铲子热、矿主冷"的资本错位——历史上，淘金热、互联网早期、云计算早期都出现过同样的形态，而最终赚最多钱的从来不是铲子，而是矿主。所以当下"芯片泡沫论"的真正含义可能不是"市场要崩"，而是"轮动还没开始"。

二、跨领域连接：margin debt 的"GDP化"思维 vs. 中央银行的债务监测逻辑

小涛对融资余额（margin debt）的处理方式非常专业——他不看绝对值，而是看 margin/指数比率 和 同比增速。这其实是把宏观经济学中"债务/GDP"的分析框架直接迁移到了散户杠杆领域。

这是一个跨领域的精妙连接： - 国家债务破历史新高 ≠ 国家危险（因为GDP也在涨） - 散户杠杆破历史新高 ≠ 市场危险（因为市值也在涨）

但他还遗漏了一个更狠的视角——信用周期理论（Minsky moment）：真正危险的不是杠杆总量，而是杠杆增速的"加速度"。 现在 margin debt 的同比增速大约30%，低于2021年的70-80%，但斜率近期在抬头。这意味着市场还没到"明斯基时刻"，但已经进入"投机性融资阶段"的早期——即借钱的人开始相信"涨势会持续"，但还没到"借钱还利息"的庞氏阶段。换句话说，派对刚刚开始进入嗨段，而不是收尾。

三、被低估的真正洞见：Cerebras 是"AI推理时代的物流公司"

小涛对 Cerebras 的分析里藏着一个被大多数散户忽略的颠覆性逻辑——HBM涨价对Cerebras是利好而非利空。这点几乎没人提。原因在于 Cerebras 不用 HBM，但竞争对手用，结果竞争对手成本上升、token涨价，反而让Cerebras可以"水涨船高"地提价。这是典型的"在通胀中受益的非通胀资产"。

更深层的跨领域连接来了——Cerebras + Groq 在AI推理生态里扮演的角色，本质上等同于物流行业里的"加急快递"： - FedEx 隔夜快递 vs 普通邮政：客户愿意为速度支付6倍溢价（小涛提到GPT-4.6快速版的token价格是标准版的6倍） - 推理速度溢价的存在，证明"AI时代的时间价值"正在被市场定价 - 当 Agent 经济（智能体替你做事）真正到来时，速度不再是"用户体验"，而是"商业可行性" —— 一个要等5分钟才响应的Agent，本质上不是产品，是玩具

最反直觉的一点：英伟达自己花200亿美金收购Groq，等于老黄亲自承认"GPU不是终极答案"。这是一个被绝大多数散户忽略的信号——英伟达正在悄悄地从"卖单一最强芯片"转型为"卖整条推理光谱"，而Cerebras正是这条光谱里"速度极端"的那一段。它的最佳客户从来不是终端用户，而是 AWS 这样的 hyperscaler——就像顺丰的最大客户从来不是个人，而是电商平台。

所以真正的"啊哈时刻"是：散户看不上Cerebras，因为他们用"替代英伟达"的框架去理解它；但市场最聪明的钱（英伟达、AWS、OpenAI）用的是"补全英伟达"的框架去配置它。这两个框架决定了完全不同的估值终点。

📝 转录文本

开场

各位观众大家好，欢迎来到一个不单只聊新闻的专业美股频道，我是市场分析员小涛。

周日、周一、周二连续三天更新，上一次连续三天我都忘了已经是什么时候了。昨天我和大家说，如果喜欢我突击更新的话，在下面留言，结果留言区爆炸了几百条留言。其实我希望大家可以保持下去，因为这样的话，其实我看留言也积极，然后大家有问题的话，我可以在留言区里面和大家继续沟通。视频本身只是一个媒介嘛，只是我们沟通的一个媒介，好吗？大家继续保持下去，然后我也继续保持下去这种势头，尽量提早发视频，然后尽量为大家多做视频。

今日内容预告

那今天我们聊什么？

  • 首先聊一下市场的走势
  • 昨天的视频里面和大家分享过三大科技股现在的一个估值在什么的个区间，一个合理的一个区间大概在什么那个位置。估值本身它是一个模糊的正确，不会给我们绝对的一个正确。今天芯片板块出现了下跌，昨天芯片板块上涨的幅度非常的好，那我们对于芯片板块整个芯片板块的估值我们再去做一个更新——这是第一部分
  • 第二部分我们去聊一下 margin debt 散户的这个杠杆线在下面的一个位置，有几个视角可以去切入的
  • 第三部分我们去聊一下周二盘后发布财报、前段时间刚刚IPO的一家芯片公司 Cerebras。这家公司其实我看完之后，我觉得是可以加入观察列表，但是我看到社交媒体上好像很多散户对于这家公司并不是非常的感冒

好吧，那我就废话不多说了，直接开始。在开始之前，大家记得留言、点赞、加转发，让更多的人可以看看到我们这期内容。

一、市场走势回顾

先来回顾一下周二资产价格的走势，那市场是出现了下跌。开盘之前相对来说会恐怖一些，那只下跌了3%，最终收盘是收到了2%，2%，还好了，就没有出现非常大幅度这个下跌。

美债和美元市场这一块的波动并不大。今天美债收入的这个下跌——周二市场美债收的这个下跌——在9月份加息的这个概率，从上周五的100%现在降到了90%左右，但还是加息的概率是非常非常的高。

三大指数

三大指数都出现了相对来说大幅度的下跌。除了纳指之外，标普下跌了1.4，纳指2.2，道指是0.1。那道指只下来了0.1，说明什么？说明整个今天的市场其实走的还好，并没有那么的差。

那标普500和纳指主要的一个跌幅是来自于芯片板块和前期大科技股的这个下跌。前七大科技股 IDD 走的是相当的弱，下跌了3.7%，而其他几乎所有的板块都出现了上涨。唯一下跌的一些板块，通讯服务也好、必需品消费也好，这两个板块其实里面的权重都是一些大科技。所以这两个板块出现了下跌，而主要这两个板块的一个下跌是因为被大科技所拖累了。

如果从结果去看，一定要去找一个理由的话，我认为可能所有的大科技全部可以归拢在一起。唯一可以找一个理由的话，现在几乎所有的大科技都在一个重的投资周期里面，那前期的这个投入非常的大，那取决于每家公司基本面的不同，回报周期可能不一样。回报周期相对比较软的、比较远的呢，市场的这个惩罚的程度相互就会大一些。

VIX：买点信号

随着标普500的下跌，盘中 VIX 去到了20，今天的收盘 VIX 是在19.49，还是在一个相对比较高的位置。我这边有两条线，两条白色的线，一条是 VIX 25 的这个位置，一条是 VIX 22 的这个位置。

特别是22的这个位置，前段时间我有在X上和大家分享过：只要我们是在一个牛市的环境里边，基本上第一个阶段到了 VIX 22 就是一个买点，接下去就是25，包括25以上，是另外一个买点。

那这时候可能一部分的观众就会问：那我们现在这一波牛市有没有结束？我个人的看法是牛市并没有结束。短期之内体感上来说，整个芯片板块好像有些泡沫，芯片和硬件好像有些泡沫，因为价格涨得非常的快、非常的高。但就像我们前面所看到的，大科技 VITD 到现在为止走势其实相当的弱，而很多大科技的估值现在也在一个相对非常低的这个位置。所以大科技这边我并不认为有任何的泡沫。

所以这是 VIX 的一个切入点：当我们在进行抄底或者加仓的时候，22是一个点位，然后25是另外一个点位。

市场参与度

市场参与度这边，虽然今天三大指数都出现了下跌，但其实参与度还是不错的。纳指超过了一半以上的公司出现了上涨，罗素2000超过了60%左右的公司出现了上涨，而标普500里面差不多也有将近60%的公司出现了上涨。所以今天前面看到几乎其实很多板块都是出现了上涨，主要指数的下跌是权重股所带领的，权重股基本上都集中在大科技和芯片板块。

所以参与度我们今天就不更新了，因为今天的参与度和昨天做对比的话，其实是有所上升的。

一年走势的反思

我们来看一下纳指在过去一年的一个走势吧。我其实不看不知道，一看的话，其实我们都在聊市场里面有个泡沫，或者担心市场里面有泡沫。但其实你去看指数的这个上涨幅度真的是不大。

上年最高纳指上年的最高点是在10月底的时候，10月底11月初的时候，当时的一个最高点到现在为止，到现在的这个点位，那只总共差不多——上年的10月份到现在要将近10个月左右的时间，8个月到现在，纳指上升了多少？纳指上升了7%。

当然了，纳指这7%里边大多数的上涨幅度全部是来自于芯片的这个上涨，也就是很多大科技现在是回到了24年的这个价格水平。所以上升了7%的这个纳指，我很难去说指数或者美股的整体现在有一个泡沫。你只能说局部可能有些泡沫，就像我前面说的，体感上你会觉得硬件板块有泡沫，是因为非常短的时间之内 SOX 芯片板块3月底到现在上涨了一倍。

但是我真的很难得出这个结论，说现在美股是在一个泡沫的阶段，没有办法完全没有办法得出这个结论。

二、芯片板块估值更新

然后是芯片板块的估值。前段时间我和大家聊的比较的多，包括我在这一次去旅游之前，也和大家分享过芯片板块的估值。我说29倍、30倍是一个可以去主动止盈的一个点。

那在昨天的时候，芯片板块 SOX 的这个估值去到了29.6、29.7倍吧，就接近于我上面白色这条线30倍的这个上区间。在30倍以上可以去主动进行止盈，那我自己手里现在第一大的仓位英特尔，我估计如果超过30倍以上，比如说去到32倍、33倍的话，SOXS、SOX 这个标的，就整个芯片如果估值去到30倍以上、32、33，我不知道非常明确的一个点位，如果到那个位置的话，我有可能会进行主动止盈我英特的仓位。当然我的这个止盈并不是我去清仓英特尔，而是主动去进行英特尔这部分的一些减仓。

美光财报前瞻

那随着周二市场的这个下跌，芯片板块的估值也从周一的29.6倍下降到了27.4倍。周三盘后会有美光的这个财报，相信美光发布完财报之后，芯片板块的这个估值——先假设发布完财报之后，美光那个价格不动，那因为财报发布完之后，财报盈利预期会向上调，那这个估值就会往下调。

所以我的一个期待是：如果我们假设明天美光的一个财报，就假设明天芯片板块没有动，发布完财报之后价格也没有动，我期待是这个估值会进行下修。

板块估值定性

所以我今天在X上又发过一条推：我说我不认为大科技是有泡沫的，大科技是低估的，然后软件板块是非常低估的，现在芯片和硬件板块的估值已经打满了。那接下去在估值打满的情况下，需要财报进来之后去修正这个估值。财报加上时间，自然就会把这个估值往下修，往下修完之后这个估值自然就会合理，就不会像现在那么高了。

那我下面画了一条线是在20倍左右。所以我认为芯片的： - 上区间：30倍 - 下区间：20倍（低于20倍就是相对会非常低估了） - 中位数：25倍

那如果说芯片的估值回到25倍，我不会认为芯片是有泡沫的，我认为芯片的估值就会回归到一个正常的区间。

三、Margin Debt：散户杠杆全景

讲到泡沫，我们来看一个视角，这个视角是 margin debt。所有 margin debt 散户这边杠杆的一个数据，在 FINRA 这个网站上是都可以找到的。

视角一：绝对体量的误区

如果我们单纯的去看体量，过去十年，从2015年到现在11年左右的时间，我们看到总共融资这个体量是一直在上升的。

上一次出现的一波上涨是什么时候？2021年的时候，就当时 Cathie Wood ARKK 非常火热的那一段时间，杠杆那段时间，2021年上升的幅度非常的快。然后22年加息这一波，整个融资的一个体量出现了下降之后，23年24年的杠杆线是加的不多的，24年、25年加上今年，杠杆上涨的幅度就非常的快。

但是这一个杠杆的体量往往会给我们一个误区：什么误区？就是随着整个市场在变得越来越大，杠杆的这个体量本身往上涨，其实不代表——比如说现在杠杆是在历史上的一个最高点，那是不是代表我们现在的一个泡沫在历史上的一个最高点？我们不能得出这样的一个结论。为什么？因为整个市场在变大。

那么就像整个经济在增速的时候，自然总体经济所承担的这个债务也会出现上升。这个债务破了历史上的一个新高，是不是代表整个经济的这个杠杆在上升？其实不代表。这就是为什么经常我们会去拿总共的一个债务除以GDP的这个比率，很多时候看这一个比率才能够看出来整个经济承担的这个债务比率。

视角二：margin debt / 指数比率

那杠杆这边也是一样的，我们需要把融资的这个体量去除以什么？比如说去除以指数等等。

还是前面那张图，只是数据源不同了，这边是 Yardeni Research。图里蓝色这条线就是我和大家前面分享的 margin debt 杠杆的总共的一个体量，红色这条线是指数。我们可以看到蓝色和红色非常的一致——指数上涨杠杆上涨，指数下跌杠杆下跌。那么现在指数破了一个新高，杠杆也破了一个新高。

但是像我前面说的，如果我们把杠杆去除以指数得出这一个比率，其实从这个比率我们可以看到现在的这个比率其实没有以前高。比如说图里灰色的这个区间就是出现衰退的这个区间，比如说08/09年的时候，我们可以看到这个比率其实非常的高，包括00年的科技泡沫也比现在高。

而相反，现在从总共的 margin debt 除以指数的这一个比率去看的话，无论和80年代当时、还是00年的科技泡沫、还是08/09年的金融危机去做对比的话，这个比率都远远低于当时的一个最高水平。

视角三：同比增速——体量

那杠杆的数据 margin debt 这个数据本身还有另外两个角度可以去切入，就是同比的这个增速，我认为也是一个非常好的切入角度。同比的增速又分为两个角度：

第一个角度就是体量——就今年和上一年去做对比，总共体量增加了多少。比如说1000亿就是 100 billion，负的1000亿就是减少了 100 billion，差不多是这样的一个概念。

所以同比我们先来看体量，体量本身也非常的有意思：

  • 我们看到在21年的时候，这是21年的3月份，整个非盈利科技公司见顶那个时间点，就当时 ARKK 见顶那个时间点，然后美股标普500是在2021年年底的时候才见底的。所以当时这些 unprofitable tech 其实是在21年年初的时候就已经见顶了。当时 margin 上升的幅度非常的快，因为很多散户都去疯狂的去购买这些标的，所以当时同比增速 margin 增加了2000亿美金

就像我前面说过，虽然现在整体市场的一个市值体量已经比21年的时候要大了很多了，将近要翻了，指数涨了多少就涨了多少嘛，翻了下半可能涨了百分之五六十了吧，23、24、25年三年市场做的非常的好，那22年是出现了下跌。

但是我们看一下，现在的同比增速的一个体量其实并没有21年要高，即使整个市场比当时的21年要高。所以从这个角度去看，起码我们和21年去做对比的话，这个体量的一个同比增速还没有21年要高。所以从疯狂的这个角度，和21年还是没有办法去做对比。

当然，我们看到历史上99年、00年当时的一个泡沫，包括08/09年当时的一个泡沫，我们同样看到了这个 margin debt 总共的一个融资体量同比增速出现了一波上涨，也就代表市场情绪面非常的高。然后从体量本身去看，当时是1000亿美金同比增速，现在我们来到了1500亿美金。现在的这个同比增速已经比08/09年和2000年要高，但是就像我前面说的，因为现在市场大了，所以这个体量本身环比的这个对比性已经没有那么大的一个参考性了。

包括和21年去做对比的话，这个参考性也不大，因为现在整个市场比21年又大了起码50%以上。

视角四：同比增速——百分比

如果说体量本身不是一个很好的环比可以去做对比的话，那我们可以看看百分比。百分比我认为就是一个比较好的衡量标准，比如说现在整个市场所承担的这个 margin 的一个体量和上年同期做对比，这个百分比到底增加了多少。百分比的话最高就是100%，对吧？所以百分比我认为是一个可以去横向作比这样的一组数据。
