item.title: [Business of Sport] It’s Money vs Prestige as the Next Generation Make Their Choice | Monty Panesar
item.dc:creator: Business of Sport
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、最反常识的观察：Monty 的"大手"是一个被忽略的隐喻系统

整段访谈中最容易被一笑而过、却最值得停下来思考的细节，是他反复提到自己的手——比 Tyson Fury 还大的手。表面上这是闲聊，但你把它和他的整段叙事并置看，会发现一种身体资本（physical capital）向品牌资本（brand capital）的隐秘转译：

  • 年轻时，"大手"是他能从左臂快投转型左臂旋的生物力学前提（biomechanics → 技术）；
  • 中年时，"大手"成了播客里被反复消费的视觉梗（biomechanics → meme）；
  • 现在，球拍赞助商 Farfire Crest 看中的不是他的技术指标，而是"你的名字一被说出来，人们就会笑"（meme → eyeballs → money）。

也就是说，他的"大手"经历了三次资产证券化：从运动器官，变成技术差异点，再变成可被赞助商定价的注意力资产。运动员退役后的真正难题，不是"再就业"，而是找到那个能在三种资本之间反复转换的底层资产。Monty 自己未必完全意识到这一点，但他在用这双手讲一个关于资产形态变化的故事。

二、跨领域连接：Roger Bannister、Ben Duckett 与"职业定价 = 公共信念的滞后函数"

他随口扔出 Roger Bannister 的例子——四分钟一英里被打破后，第二年有 20 个人也跑进去了。这是个体育掌故，但它实际上在解决一个经济学问题：

一个职业的"价值"，不是由它的内在难度决定的，而是由集体相信"这件事值得做"的人数决定的。

把这个框架套回他真正想说的事——测试板球 vs T20——画面立刻变了：

  • T20 不是"打败"了测试板球，而是把"年轻人愿意为之牺牲十年"这件事，重新定价到了一个测试板球付不起的水平；
  • Ben Duckett 拒绝 IPL 选测试，看起来是"情怀"，但用 Bannister 框架看，他其实是在赌一个反向 Bannister 时刻——赌未来某天人们会重新相信"测试板球手"这个标签更稀缺、更高级。这是一笔逆向资产配置。
  • 这也解释了 Monty 那句被很多人会读成怀旧的话："people remember test cricketers"。他不是在伤春悲秋，他是在做长期持有的论证。

跨领域看，这和学术界的 tenure 制度、独立电影 vs 流媒体、长篇小说 vs 短视频是同构的问题：当短周期回报曲线变陡，谁来为"慢资产"的稀缺性买单？Monty 提出的解法（ICC 把 T20 收入抽税回流给小国测试赛）——本质上是一个文化资产的转移支付机制，和欧盟农业补贴的逻辑没有任何区别。

三、最容易被错过的洞见：Monty 把 Gary Lineker 的话听反了，而这恰恰是他的天才

Lineker 给他的那句忠告——"当人们不再找你签名，你就不再 relevant"——Monty 把它当作座右铭挂在嘴边。但仔细听他全程讲的故事，你会发现他事实上反向操作了这条建议：

Lineker 的逻辑是：relevance = 被认出（pull from public） Monty 的实际做法是：relevance = 我让对方感觉良好（push to public）

注意他描述边界 fielding 时和酒后球迷开玩笑、跑到 Barmy Army 面前欢呼、club cricket 里只为击中一次 off stump 就能"撑起整个夏天"——这些都不是在"维持知名度"，而是在做情感供给。他真正的资产不是被认出，而是被认出之后那个人离开时的感受。

这有个非常具体的商业含义：他的"second innings"之所以在英国不温不火、在印度反而做得起来，可能不是因为媒体市场结构问题，而是因为英国媒体生态奖励 opinion（Vaughn、Swann 式的尖锐评论），而 Monty 的真正天赋是 warmth——这是两种完全不同的注意力变现路径。他自己也许还在错误地试图模仿前者的路径，却没意识到自己手里其实握着稀缺得多的后者。

跨领域类比：这和米其林厨师转型做家庭料理 IP 是一回事——技术资产和情感资产是两套商业模型，硬把后者装进前者的渠道里，永远会觉得"差一点"。他自己已经说出了那个未竟之问："请来告诉我，你作为粉丝喜欢我哪一点"——这句话本身就是答案：他需要的不是更多曝光，而是更准确的自我定位反馈回路。

📝 转录文本

开场金句

"I think a lot of young cricketers — they're thinking it's a short career, I'm going to make more money being a T20 cricketer. But people remember test cricketers, and I think now cricketers are beginning to see that."

"When I meet a lot of people, they said: what you did is you actually changed the complete landscape of people within the Sikh community, because it massively opened doors in terms of breaking the ice — which I didn't realize at the time, because I just love playing cricket for England."

"I remember the likes of Gary Lineker — he said to me: the moment people stop asking for your autograph and your picture, stop recognizing you, it means you are irrelevant."

"In British sport, when you have those impossible moments where people just think it's going to get out in the next 10, 15 minutes — when you take one ball at a time, it's interesting how far it can take you. And next thing I know, I'm shaking Ricky Ponting's hand and we've drawn a test match."

"And I'm still waiting for my, you know, OBE — I haven't received it yet. Or maybe even a knighthood. Would that be better?" — "Knighthood would be better." — "Yeah, it may be harder." — "It would be harder." — "Should we start OBE? Yeah, OBE. Let's stick with OBE."

欢迎与开场：体育与商业的关联

Host: Fascinating. Monty Panesar — welcome to the Business of Sport. Lovely to have you in.

Monty: Yeah, thank you for having me on the show. I'm absolutely delighted to be on here. I remember when I first finished from cricket, a lot of people in the city — wherever I go, to events or meeting people at networking events — they go: is there a correlation between sports and business? Can a sportsman's mindset enhance a company, make the directors, the CEO perform better? Or a sportsman being part of that team just enhances and uplifts? And a lot of people said to me there's massive correlations between sports and business.

Host: So is this something that you've always been interested in then? You played many years at the top of the game.

Monty: I always had that interest when I used to go and meet people. The biggest thing for me was — I remember the likes of Gary Lineker. He said to me: "The moment people stop asking for your autograph and your picture, stop recognizing you, it means you are irrelevant." And it always stuck with me, that advice.

So I asked him, because he said to me — I said to him: "You made a successful career after playing football, a media career which is equally successful, probably even better now, right? Especially with the growth of Goalhanger" — obviously, more great podcasts — "exactly, and that's your inspiration." So you kind of think, well, let's follow the footsteps of other people who have done really well — the likes of Mark Nicholas, Graeme Swann is another one who has done really, really well, Michael Vaughan is good, Nasser Hussain, Kathryn Turner.

Host: Well, you were on Stick to Cricket recently, right? And if you look at that setup — Bumble, Michael Vaughan, Alastair Cook…

Monty: And I forgot — yeah, Phil Tufnell.

Host: Yeah, Alastair Cook, Phil — all of them lot. It's been a brilliant transition for them where they've now got a media personality that complements what they were also doing on a performance side. But I'd also imagine it's such a competitive environment for you to get into and stand out in.

Monty: Yeah, it is. It's very competitive, and it's an area I want to go into. Obviously I done an MA in sports journalism, and once I graduated, opportunities came — I was writing regularly for the Telegraph quite a bit, on TalkSport, bit on Sky, and then eventually I got more work in India as a commentator. Now the challenge for me is how can I get more presence in the UK — media opportunities, business opportunities. That's one of the reasons I reach out to you guys.

There is a massive correlation between the elite mindset of an athlete going into business, but also: can the second innings be equally successful as the first? That's always a challenge for a sportsperson. When I walk around London or wherever I am, I am a brand myself. Going back to what Lineker said, if I get people to recognize me, taking autographs, the popularity is there — that means I'm relevant. I'm very relevant in the media, entertainment world, in all of that kind of space.

作为英格兰首位锡克教徒板球手

Host: It's really important in this day and age, isn't it, to have that. I mean, you were the first Sikh to play for England, right? A test team — correct?

Monty: Yeah. The first to play outside of India, I think — not for the Indian test team.

Host: Having that type of moment, having that type of relationship, is such an important part of you being able to stand out and inspire in ways that other people just maybe won't be able to. How do you build something around that, that is often quite challenging?

Monty: It's a really good point. You talk about being the first Sikh to play — never thought of that, but within the Sikh community, people are very proud. When I meet a lot of people, they said: "Monty, what you did is you actually changed the complete landscape of people within the Sikh community." When they go to work, they'll say, "Oh, Monty's here," or if they wear a patka, or got a 'Singh' name behind it — they go, "I'm a Sikh." And it just opened doors — it massively opened doors in terms of breaking the ice, having a laugh instead of just talking normally. It'll be like, "Yeah, you're Monty, you know, playing cricket for England."

A lot of people in the Sikh community say that to me. They go, "That was a massive pivotal moment" — which I didn't realize at the time, because I just love playing cricket for England. That was my passion. I didn't think about anything else. I just want to play cricket for England, become the best I can be, and win games for my country.

But when these opportunities come — the memories of going out to Lord's, there's 30,000 people cheering, you know your name… It could be one person and a dog — you're irrelevant. Your commodity, your value, are the fans — how you make them feel. The fans don't care about your wickets or runs; it's the interaction. When they first meet you, they'll think, "Oh my god, I had such a great experience meeting Monty," or some other great sportsman — Cristiano Ronaldo, Leo Messi. Obviously I'm not in their bracket, right? But what I'm trying to say is they take away that. They won't take away how good your bowling was. It's how you made them feel. And that's, after retirement, your commodity as a sportsperson: how you make that fan feel. If you make them feel great, that means you're valued. And opportunities keep coming.

与球迷的关系：Fine Leg 上的香槟与玩笑

Host: As a fan, I can say it seemed like fans had an amazing relationship with you that stood out beyond a lot of other things I saw. Did you feel that as a player? Did you feel that relationship with the fans when you were playing?

Monty: Yeah, there were times — I'll be down at fine leg after tea, and the guys are probably on the fifth glass of champagne, and they're telling me how to field or how to catch a ball. They go to me, "Make sure you get your two pinky fingers together, and when it comes underneath, it's going to come, mate. You're going to take a great catch." Or if I misfielded it, they go, "Don't worry about it, try again."

I began to then play on that. I began to have a laugh with them, have a bit of banter. If I took a wicket, I'd go down to fine leg and really cheer them on. And when it was going really well, even my teammates, the England teams, would say, "Go on, go down to the Barmy Army, go cheer them up a little bit when you take a wicket." And I did. That energy helped us to perform really well.

There are ways of keeping the fans on your side. If you get them on your side, it somehow elevates you and people just want to talk to you. I try and make as much time as I can — because without the support of the fans, I think sportsmen's commodity isn't as high. It's the love and the blessings from the fans that takes you there.

从 Luton 到英格兰：进入板球的路径

Host: Completely. What was your pathway to cricket?

Monty: As a youngster, as a 10-year-old, I played for Luton Town. That club amalgamated with Luton Town and Indians, and then I played for Dunstable Town as well to play a better standard of cricket. Then I had my coach David Mercer — he was a sports, sorry, cricket development officer. At the time the ECB had just started to allocate spots as development officer for every county, and he pushed me quite a lot.

Then I remember Paul Taylor at the age of 15 said: "Well, I think you should give this a go, because you're not going to become a left-arm seamer." And he was a great left-arm seamer for Northants. I recently saw him at the Northants reunion. He didn't realize at the time — I thought, well, I'm only going to tell him to bowl left-arm spin because it'll be better for him. He didn't realize that piece of advice would be the turning point of my life.

The next game I took 7 for 35 against Worcester Under-15s. A year later I was playing minor counties cricket, men's cricket. At the age of 17, Northants and Essex wanted to sign me.

Host: How did you pick it up that quickly? Because it's very technical.

Monty: Because when you're bowling all the time — I was an opening bowler — your line and length is good. This time you're just imparting spin. So you got the biomechanics there. Very quickly, Northants had John Emburey, Nick Cook there; Essex had John Childs — all spin coaches and good spinners themselves. I was very lucky.

I got through the system, accelerated very quickly. But if that moment didn't happen — Paul Taylor telling me — I'm sure people ask me in life, when I ask CEOs or business people, there would have been that moment where if someone's given you that right piece of advice, it's like… The Matrix: do I take the red pill or the blue pill? You make that decision and suddenly you're going in a different direction. That was my moment. I always thank Paul Taylor for just giving me that piece of advice — it changed my life.

Talent can take you so far, but a piece of advice from a mentor — someone who's been there — can actually elevate you to the next level. I think that's the lucky break that everyone looks for, and that was mine: when he told me, "Don't be a left-arm seamer, become a left-arm spinner."

大手、生物力学，与 Tyson Fury

Host: There's a big gap between that and then playing for England, right? So you must have picked that up and been pretty effective in those years that followed.

Monty: Yes. Biomechanically — I had big hands, I had big shoulders.

Host: They said this on the —

Monty: Yeah. So look, your hands are — oh my god, my hands are bigger than yours. I didn't realize. And you got big hands — and you've got big hands — and mine are bigger than yours.

Host: That's ridiculous.

Monty: I measured my hand against Tyson Fury — and they're as big as his.

Host: So when the guy said it on Stick to Cricket, I was listening. I was like, "Okay." I mean, how big are they?

Monty: Yeah. So maybe I should go into boxing.

Host: I was going to say —

Monty: Look at these fists.

Host: Influencer boxing's huge now. So ex-cricketer boxing — who would you fight?

Monty: Wow. I don't want to call anyone out — they could be asking me for a fight, so I'm going to be careful here.

Host: Well, Sachin was your first test wicket, right? So he could be your first fight.

Monty: No, no, I could never. Sachin is a great human being on and off the field, a role model for cricket. He's up there as one of the greatest sportsmen of all time. I was very lucky — there's moments where you think, you're just on the same field. I think that's an achievement. I could tell my grandchildren: I was on the same field as one of the greatest sportsmen of all time. But to get his first test wicket and then regularly bowl to him and compete — it was just a dream come true. I could have pinched myself for these kind of moments.

Host: It was a great wicket. I fell into a bit of a hole before this just reminding myself of some of your bowling. Maybe we'll put it in the video. It was great. That pad onto bat — literally right in front. Good decision from the umpire.

Monty: I always thank him for it. He was quick.

Host: Yeah, he immediately gave it up. And even looking at it now, it looked pretty prom.

Monty: It was a brilliant moment of my career.

处子秀：在印度，对阵 Sachin、Dravid、Jaffer

Host: How was it for you to start your England career in India, on that tour?

Monty: I remember at the time Andrew Flintoff named the side, and I was the last name. I thought to myself, "I must be 12th man." Spinners are like that — they just don't believe in themselves as much sometimes. Even though they have to win a game of cricket, they always need a bit of boosting and pumping up. When there's like the last day of a test match, you have everyone around the bat, you're creating the theatre, but you're always bouncing off the teammates' energy.

When he announced, I was like, "Oh — am I 12th man?" He goes, "No, you're playing tomorrow." I quickly went running upstairs in my room in the hotel and did the field settings.

Before then, I was on the Darren Lehmann Academy in 2005 in Adelaide. He always said to me: "Make sure you bowl a yard fuller than what you do in England or in Australia. You got to be more fuller than shorter." He also said: "Make sure you write your field settings and give it to the coach or the captain, because you're not going to be hearing — when we went out there, I could not hear. You're signaling, you're looking for signals, you're thinking what's going on, and you could not hear what the captain is saying."

So I quickly gave him the field settings: I want a man on the drive. I want a short leg. I want point just to be 5 yards behind, because if it turns then the fielder can catch it there. I'll have cover. Got mid-on, mid-on halfway, because I know the Indian batsmen, they whack it really hard — so if you put it halfway, create a bit of doubt. Then midwicket I want a bit straighter, and square leg deep square leg I want, obviously, there. So the gap there's a bit — so it can play across the line a little bit. That's all it was.

I thought to myself: how am I going to bowl to the likes of Mohammad Kaif, Sachin Tendulkar, Wasim Jaffer who was playing in that test match, Rahul Dravid — the great Rahul Dravid — MS Dhoni? And the list just keeps going on and on. You're like, "Am I even ready for this?" But I've worked all my life — so this is the mindset that goes into your head when you get to that destination: I don't know if I'm ready for it, but let's give it a go. I've trained all my life for this.

I'm going to have three lions on my shirt, 631, and I'm going to sing the national anthem, and I'm going to see flags of Luton, Bedford, all the places around Bedfordshire, areas I know. And that's when it becomes real. You're singing to the national anthem and you're thinking, "Oh my god, I'm representing my country." Which you always dream about — but when it becomes real, you're still pinching yourself. You think, "Maybe I'm living a dream. This is not really happening."

Host: Yeah. It would be a dream for me. So, it will forever be a dream for me. I'll take your word for it.

Monty: Exactly. But you did it 50 times, right? And did it always hold every time you stepped onto that pitch? Did it always hold that special?

It got easier as you did it more. And then it got to a point where — sports psychologists say this — once that reality becomes the norm, then you got to say to yourself, "I'm five days at the office now." If I go to Lord's, get myself ready, go to other cricketing venues — Old Trafford, Headingley, Edgbaston — it's a day at the office. That's all I'm doing. Then it becomes like a normal job. You get into your spell, you work out the conditions.

In hindsight, I could have played maybe 20 test matches more if I didn't have my wear and tear. I could have taken a few more wickets. But you have your highs and lows. You can't regret. No point crying over spilled milk now. But whatever I achieved — yeah, I'm very lucky.

2009 灰烬系列赛：与 Jimmy Anderson 守平 Cardiff Test

Host: Sport's a game of moments. Quite often, we talked about the importance of brand. In team games in particular, it's that goal that you score that stands out in memory; it's the wicket you take. But I do feel for you — there was that moment in 2009 in the Ashes series where you and Jimmy Anderson batted out to rescue the test for England. When that happened, did you come out of it recognizing there had been a shift in attention, demand for you, interest in you?

Monty: Yeah, very much so. In British sport, when you have those impossible moments where people just think, "Well, Monty's coming out — he's going to get out in the next 10, 15 minutes" — when you take one ball at a time, it's interesting how far it can take you. It's the same thing in life as well. In business, you just focus on each meeting, each day at a time. You don't focus six months ahead — yes, you have your goals set, but if you're sitting today and thinking where do we want to be in six months, you don't end up getting there. If you just focus one day at a time, one hour at a time, every meeting at a time — one ball at a time — you can actually get there quicker.

That's what happened. I just focused on one ball at a time. And next thing I know, I'm shaking Ricky Ponting's hand and we've drawn a test match. That was a special moment in English cricket.

Host: Does it almost make it easier sometimes if you have that pressure? As you said, everyone was just expecting you to get out. You probably perform better for it.

Monty: Absolutely. It made it a lot easier for me, because I knew what my challenge was: to bat there as long as I can and try and draw the test match for England. I knew there were two shots I was going to play: if it's at the stumps, protect the stumps. If it's short, just drop the hands down. And if it hits me wherever it does, let it hit you — but don't let it hit your gloves. So two very simple instructions. Instead of looking at the long-term picture — "Oh, there's 45 minutes to go" — you just break it down ball by ball, ball by ball, incremental, small steps.

There was a moment where you're thinking we're getting closer and closer. When Michael North came on to bowl — he's a part-time off-spinner — I thought, "I've got every chance here now." He's a good spinner, and obviously he's England selector now, but back in that time he was probably a good batsman and part-time off-spin. You have that confidence: Ricky Ponting is now bringing on his part-time bowler. He's not bringing on his frontline bowlers. You have that confidence to think, "Maybe we're going to get much more closer to drawing this test match."

And I'm still waiting for my OBE — I haven't received it yet.

Host: Well, King Charles.

Monty: I need to email King Charles and say, "When do I get my OBE for drawing the test match?"

Host: People have got them for less.

Monty: A lot of people have got it for a lot less.

Host: We can start the campaign.

Monty: Or maybe even knighthood. Would that be better?

Host: Knighthood would be better.

Monty: Yeah, it may be harder.

Host: It would be harder.

Monty: Should we start OBE?

Host: Yeah, OBE. Let's stick with OBE. Start with OBE.

Monty: Maybe I need to do more other stuff to get knighted and be known as "Sir Monty." Maybe.

灰烬系列赛的历史与传承

Host: How was it playing in the Ashes series?

Monty: It was amazing. The history that goes behind it — I remember the times when Steve Waugh scored 100 at the Oval on one leg. I remember when Ian Botham single-handedly won test matches for England. Then I saw Graham Gooch play some phenomenal innings. Darren Gough's hat-tricks — these are the memories I've had of England doing well. Michael Vaughan scoring those back-to-back hundreds in Australia. Andrew Flintoff — 2005 Ashes was his series, doing so well. Kevin Pietersen scoring unbelievably, his first 100. There's great memories, and you'll kind of think to yourself: you are part of that list as well of great memories of Ashes cricket. With Jimmy Anderson on the other side as well, and now look how well he's done. It gives you huge pride and satisfaction.

进入精英梯队的路径：从 ECB 的大学网点到 Northants

Host: I can imagine. Tell me about pathways and opportunities to hit the top of the game. Because one side of sport, unless it's football, is the feasibility of committing to careers in sport. In cricket it's slightly different — you'll have your central contracts for England, you'll also have your county contracts. When you were coming through, how much of that thought process in your head was, "This is something I'm going to commit to as a job," versus only ever focusing on what it really meant to you, and you'd make all of that work at a later date?

Monty: When I was younger, I remember I wrote a letter to Bedford Modern, you know, cricket master, just to see if I can get in the sixth form and try and play there as an opportunity. So Nigel Chinneck got me a cricket scholarship there. That really helped me for two years playing there.

Then we had the BIS — the cricket development officer David Mercer — who helped me in terms of getting into the minor counties team. I played minor counties. Close by, I had Northants as my pathway system. But then ECB introduced the UCCE — university cricket centres. There were six centres at the time: Cambridge, Oxford, Exeter, Cardiff, Durham, Loughborough. I applied for my course at Loughborough University because of the facilities. I got in to do computing and management. Then I got the best of both worlds — studying as well as playing cricket.

If the ECB never introduced that — would I have gone to university or not? Probably the answer would be no, because at the time Northants said: "Out of 100 cricketers, I would say you probably will make it. So I'd rather you not go to university and focus on your career here." But I said I don't mind actually still going to university and giving it my best shot, and hopefully I can catch up after that.

With that in mind, I got opportunities of both. That gave me a bit more time — during June, July, second half of the season, wickets turn a bit more. So when I come back from university, I can play straight, hopefully into the first team. That's what happened. At the age of 23 in my final year, played eight games, took 51 or 53 wickets. Quickly the likes of Geoff Miller, David Graveney — selectors at the time — had an eye on me straight away. They thought, "Yeah, this guy can probably play for England, he's performing really well." I was taking big wickets — Graeme Hick, Andy Flower, who are good players of spin. That's how your name gets mentioned, because selectors look not at the wickets you take, but the big players. If you're taking regular wickets of them, you get noticed more.

Gradually I got into that system. So I thank ECB massively for creating those pathways.

县级合同 vs 国家队：财务可行性

Host: And if you hadn't played for England — let's say just on a county contract — how feasible was that for you from a financial perspective, business perspective?

Monty: You could still earn good money. But if I was just county cricket, then it would not give me the satisfaction of where I can go, as I'm trying to push myself and stretch myself to become the best cricketer I can be.

You get a lot of county cricketers who maybe sometimes feel they may not believe they can play for England, and then they start working on other careers, having businesses on the side as well as playing cricket. During the winter months they will maybe develop their own cricket academies or start developing another career — knowing that if I develop this over the winter months and then play cricket during the summer, I'm busy both times of the year. While others, they'll be like, "I want to play for England, so I'm going to push myself, go away and play in Australia, in South Africa, better my game, come back and really go there." It's up to the individual.

Host: When you play for England, do you feel like an England player who maybe drops in to play a bit of county cricket? Because that's the interesting thing about cricket. Normally — let's apply it to football — you're a club footballer who occasionally goes and plays an international match. But it does seem from the outside like when England are playing, the majority of your cricket's pretty much played for England.

Monty: Absolutely. I played a lot of cricket — 50 matches for England. But it was very much early on in my England career I was playing more games for England, less for county cricket. Then after the Cardiff test — that was my last home test match — it was the other way: I was playing more first-class cricket for Sussex and Essex, and less games for England.

Host: That was your last home test match?

Monty: That was my last home test match. Yeah.

Host: Wow. And was that because, while traveling, you were more effective on the pitches internationally?

Monty: Yeah, that as well. And then obviously another guy called Graeme Swann came in the way — a good spinner, good batter, good slip catcher, and probably a better bloke as well in the dressing room. A funny guy with all his accents he can bring in. He's a good after-dinner speaker, and he's done very well in the media world as a commentator. So how can you compete? I had no choice but to sit down and make his drinks and say, "There you go, mate. What do you want as a 12th man?"

退役过渡：从更衣室到媒体教室

Host: Tell us, if you're happy to, about when you kind of get to the end of that England career — what happens? How do you deal with those transitions as an athlete?

Monty: As an athlete you build resilience, you build discipline, you build that winning mindset, you build about how to work in a team. If someone drops a catch, you don't have a go at them — you go up to them and say, "Don't worry, try the next one a bit harder," and they'll end up trying 20–30% harder, they'll end up taking a blinder. But if you go at them hard, they're going to be nervous. So you learn emotional intelligence — there's many things you learn: drive, focus, and all these other things.

Once I stopped playing, my autobiography came out, and I started to put my feet in the media world. I remember my parents — they said, "If you're serious about working in the media, you should go and do a course." I quickly went, thought which is closest to London. St Mary's University was there. I started doing it, created my little podcast called Monty Meets You as my final-year project. I didn't manage to push that further. That was a great experience for me, and things kept on pushing further.

Host: How long were you playing cricket for after you played your last match for England? How long did you keep going?

Monty: I kept on going, playing club cricket. Played in Liverpool for Wally, Hornchurch. Played at Great Witchingham. Now I play at Pelsall Cricket Club at South Staffordshire Premier League. I still love playing cricket — it keeps me fit, but I love just being part of a cricket club to understand the landscape of cricket at grassroots level. It keeps me connected.

板球商业版图：私有化、IPL、与百球赛

Host: How do you view the business of cricket today? We look at multi-formats. Everyone's very focused on short form. We know there's a threat to the longer-form game. You played most of your cricket in the longer-form game, and didn't venture as much — I know you did partially — but as much into that kind of big franchise cricket world. Without putting any words in your mouth, if I'm just asking you to take a look at the cricket landscape today — what do you see?

Monty: The way I view it is there's a lot of privatization going on. Look at the Hundred — 50% of the teams have been sold already to the IPL owners. Where the big money is, they'll come in. Eventually you'll see, like, MI London — Mumbai Indians.

Host: Super Giants Manchester.

Monty: Sunrisers Leeds.

Host: Yeah, Sunrisers Leeds.

Monty: Eventually it's going to be like a T20 game format really. The Hundred, I think, from the men's cricket, will change to a T20 format. And you'll see wider audiences — you may see a lot more people from the South Asian community going to the cricket grounds because they may see some Indian cricketers coming. A lot more money will come. You may see bhangra, which is a Sikh form of music — performers going in halfway during a match like the NFL with rappers. Same thing with bhangra singers going in there. A different form of entertainment coming into English cricket.

But it's helping, isn't it? It's keeping a lot of counties afloat. There's a lot of money. Yorkshire's debt is being cleared off because of that money. The same is happening with the Big Bash — they're going to play their first game in Chennai at the start of the Big Bash season. India is the hub of it. India has the grip and the control on world cricket, and that's where the money is at, really.

Host: I was going to say, when you said, "I'm seeing a lot of privatization," it almost sounded like you didn't agree with it.

Monty: No, I agree with it, because the game has to grow and get bigger. But I also want — hopefully these leagues that are being owned by investors and private companies, there's a forethinking: we have some responsibility in making sure test cricket still survives. Test cricket needs to survive, and they've got to do everything they can. Maybe the ICC can get hold of it where they say, "Look, a percentage of the money goes into a fund of just helping other nations with match fees, with TV rights" — whatever it is to make sure that the boards, the smaller nations, they're not losing money. There's a lot of money being made in these leagues — but maybe some parts of it can be contributed towards test cricket.

Host: I recognize I'm anglifying this, but hey, we're in England. For test cricket to survive, we need the county system to thrive. So it's interesting that through the creation of the Hundred — a short-form game — you've now seen huge amounts of money. Yorkshire, I think, received between 50 to 60 million for their stake. They were the only county to sell their whole stake, but that money has enabled the county to survive, clear the debts, and invest. So these short-form games — while it's a kind of weird imbalance in a way, because they're taking time, calendar space, talent away from long-form cricket — at the same time they're providing the key cash needed to keep these 150-year-old assets afloat.

Monty: Look, that's up to the owners — the owners of the teams, the owners of these leagues. We see Big Bash and IPL — they have that mindset to think, "We're making a lot of money owning these T20 teams, and we also got to make sure there's that window for test cricket for the nations." You don't want gradually the numbers reducing. England are playing probably 12 to 14 test matches this year, but other nations are only playing five or six — not many. You don't want to get to a point where there's fewer numbers with the smaller nations, and only the slightly bigger ones like India, Australia, and England end up playing the majority of test cricket, while the others drop down and just keep focusing on shorter formats. It's really important to think: is there a minimum number of test matches the smaller nations have to play? That will be the way to maintain test cricket.

假如你打的是 T20 时代：自我估价与"大六码"

Host: How would you have looked at your value if you were playing top-level franchise cricket?

Monty: Yeah, I think I'll be worth £1 million pounds.

Host: Do you? Yeah, I agree. You bowled quite fast, didn't you, for a spinner?

Monty: Yeah, I bowled fast and I bowled it into the pitch quite quickly. Someone bowling the powerplay, a couple of overs — maybe have to bowl it into the pitch quickly. I'll have to improve my fielding a lot, be a lot more athletic. I would learn how to hit big sixes. I'll employ a power coach to learn how to hit 100-metre sixes. That's all feasible once you know what skills are required.

Wasn't it Roger Bannister, right? Yeah, the four-minute mile — when he broke it, the following year 20 other people broke it. It's the same thing. You evolve. If right now you're a young batsman, you are assessed on: can you hit 100-metre sixes — how far can you hit a six? It doesn't matter about your defense. As a bowler: how quickly can you spin a cricket ball and get it to turn? What is the maximum speed you can bowl a spin ball, and it turns? This could be maybe 60 mph or 65, and that's where it's at. Then from there you start playing around with it. You bowl the slower one, you bowl the quicker one. But you got to have the ability. The successful spinners in T20 cricket are the ones who bowl it quick and get it to turn.

Host: Yeah. What do you think of Suri? He's not bad, is he?

Monty: Unbelievable. He is the prime example of — if I'm coaching young cricketers and the parents come up to me, they got a 10-year-old kid, thinking in five years' time, "I want my child to play in the T20 blast in the Hundred." That's the dream now that's being sold to cricket. It's accelerated growth. Everybody wants growth quickly in a short amount of time. That's going to be impossible maybe at 15 — but this is how parents are thinking.

Host: What's interesting is we've been lucky enough to speak to a few top-level cricketers, and every single time — I don't know, maybe it is PR because it's the thing they think people want to hear, but I genuinely believe it — you go, "What's the pinnacle for you?" and all of them say, "I'd give up short form in a heartbeat if I still got to play test cricket." So to this current group of players, test cricket remains that pinnacle. The challenge, as you said, is for the generation coming through — whether they can retain that value in test cricket, or whether it is seeing the 15-year-old walk out in the IPL in front of the fanfare. The crowds, the atmosphere — it's what every sportsperson wants to play in. If that's where you get it and you get that level of fanfare and exposure, it's hard not to aspire to achieve that.

Monty: Look, I agree with you. If I had a child right now and he was a 10-year-old, I'd be thinking, "In five years' time, can I make him into the next Suri?" That is what people want. They want accelerated growth.

But you got to also learn your cricket, right? Where I play my club cricket, Pelsall Cricket Club, we have 50-over games. Then we have the red-ball format where you bowl 15 overs. You learn how to bowl your stock delivery — the bread and butter of cricket is the red-ball format. Then we go to playing T20 cricket. It's really important at grassroot levels, even at second XI, even at school level, that there is some form of red-ball cricket being played, because then the young cricketers — 11, 12-year-olds, or even 14, 15 — they're going to learn to play red-ball format, which is really important. Then after that, yeah, you'll branch out as you get older. You think, "Okay, I'll sign this 18-year-old, 19-year-old because he'd be a good little T20 cricketer. He can whack a few sixes, bowl a little bit."

As a bowler, if you're a spinner, you don't really need to learn your trade — if you can bowl two, three overs quite handsomely, pick up a couple of wickets, be a little bit unpredictable in your lines and lengths. As a batsman, hit a few sixes — you can get signed up. Before, 20 years ago, it was: how good are they? How good is their stock delivery? How good are they in red-ball format? If you was good in red-ball format, you'll get signed up. Now they see it as: "Oh, this bits-and-pieces cricketer — how does he fit into my T20 team?" And then they can learn on the way how to become a better red-ball format.

Host: So with that in mind, do you fear for the future of spin bowling?

Monty: No, because spin bowlers are always going to have an impact in T20 cricket. But what you can do — if you have a good youngster who is bowling it quickly and turning it and picking up wickets in T20 cricket, but when you put them into a red-ball format, they'll probably have to bowl a bit slower because the shape on the ball has to be better. Slightly different attributes, but you can teach them that. Coaches now are thinking, "Ooh, he could bowl it quick. Can he bat? He's a good fielder. Let's sign him — he'll be a good signing for a T20, maybe get to the Hundred as well — and then you can teach him the red-ball stuff."

球员品牌、女子板球与全球扩张

Host: What do you think it's done for the brand of overall cricket?

Monty: I think it's grown massively. It's gone to different global spaces we never thought it would be able to. We have so many different leagues now all over the world — European leagues happening, leagues in Canada, in the West Indies, South Africa. SA T20 is a prime example of why South African cricket is so strong in T20 and ODI formats — because their domestic T20 game is so strong.

That's what's going to happen with England as well. They will get closer to India. Look at the last T20 World Cup — India and England, when they played in that semi-final, England were always behind the game. Even though Jacob Bethell scored that magnificent 100, they were always behind. They were never ahead of India. That's where I think the difference will be. They'll get closer to India, closer to Australia, because of the domestic tournament. When the Hundred will be a much stronger format —

Host: Tymal Mills said the South African T20 was his favourite that he's played in. He's played in all of — he's played the IPL, he's played the Hundred here. He said it was just something about the atmosphere around it. And obviously being in South Africa in the middle of summer is not a bad thing to be either. But that kind of surprised him a little bit, how much he enjoyed it.

Monty: Yeah, because the fan following — what Graeme Smith has done is brilliant there. He's kind of made sure all the crowds are packed and the players get good value for the experience of playing there. But also the wickets — the wickets are going to help fast bowlers more. So when he comes in and bowls quick, he'll get more bounce and more seam movement. So he'll always feel he's in the game. While if he's maybe in India, he'll be thinking, "Well, I hope I don't get hit for a big six here, even though I'm bowling it quick. How do I avoid myself getting hit for a boundary?" In South Africa you'll probably get more seam movement and more swing as well.

英格兰板球的当下：选材、风格、与旋球的位置

Host: How do you feel with English cricket at the moment? It's a very strange relationship between fans and English cricket right now. Performance, obviously, in the Ashes was far from ideal — particularly with a lot of the hope going into it. But it's been so much built on a style and a way of playing and a culture and a mentality, and then what it's done for some of the players that have been selected and types of players that have been left out. Spin has been a really interesting thing that has fallen by the wayside in this era — if you want to call it an era. How do you look at it holistically amongst all of the opinions that everyone's throwing out from a cricketing perspective?

Monty: From a cricketing perspective, if you want to get in the England team, you got to understand: what are the requirements for getting in the England team, and where do you fit in? Where is the spot for me that I have to fit in?

If you're a young county cricketer, that's how you're going to look at it. And if you're a young county cricketer, I would go — even if you get an opportunity — and speak to an England selector. Go and reach out. If there's ever a time Marcus North is going to watch a county match, go and speak to him: "Look, what do I need to do to get in the England team?" Then you get the proper low-down on what you got to do.

In my opinion, the way the England team are playing right now — if you're a batsman, yeah, you got to have a good solid defense, but you also got to learn to score quickly. It's no point scoring a thousand runs and your strike rate is at 35–40. They're looking at higher strike rates, maybe 60–65. As a batsman, you got to look at the numbers. Someone like Harry Brook — that 40-odd he scored in the first innings against New Zealand was probably as valuable as 100, but he did it very quickly. He took on the attack. He played positive. That's what England are looking at — when England bat — but in a bit untouchable: do you have the ability to do that?

As a seamer, do you have the ability to actually bowl well when the captain sets attacking, ultra-attacking fields? You're not a defensive bowler. You're happy to get hit for a few runs as well as take wickets. And from a spinner's point of view, how do you get into that team? I always believe as a spinner, your stock delivery's got to be really good. If your stock delivery is good, then you got a chance, because you got to somehow convince the captain and the coach to keep me on. If your stock delivery isn't good and they keep hitting you four, five runs an over, then the captain's going to think spin may not work in English conditions. But it can work if you're a good spinner and you have a good stock delivery.

Host: Do you think the lack of utilization of spin over the last few years for England really is down to people not grabbing the opportunity and showcasing this leadership that spin needs to play a key part in — versus it's just not part of the tactics of this group? Or is it a combination?

Monty: It's a combination. And it's also: if I'm a young spinner and I'm thinking I can hit a few sixes, and it doesn't take that much out of my body — do I really want to play county cricket? I can earn more money playing franchise cricket. If I can get into the T20 Blast, get with a Hundred franchise, if I can play white-ball format for England and then back of that get into the IPL, maybe Big Bash, PSL — all the other leagues around. As an agent, they're probably thinking, "You're going to make more money doing this. Then actually bowling 500 overs in county cricket and try and learn the traditional way — you're not going to make as much money."

So a lot of young cricketers are looking at it from that perspective. They're thinking it's a short career. "I'm going to make more money being a T20 cricketer. It doesn't matter — it's a test number, I'm not going to make as much money." But people remember test cricketers. Because test cricket — if you're good at test cricket, it means you're good at other formats. And I think now cricketers are beginning to see that.

Ben Duckett is a prime example. He's thinking: "I don't want to play in the IPL. I want to play county cricket because I want to be known as a good test cricketer — not someone who was good in T20 cricket." That's where I feel that in the long term, we may even see the boards increasing more money — so they can retain the good players to play test cricket rather than going to become a T20 franchise.

But if you're that sort of test cricketer where — if you're like a Will Jacks, for example — where you're good at striking a ball, bowl a little bit, but you know you're not going to guarantee a spot in the England team unless it's a turning pitch and you come and play as a second spinner, then you're going to think, "Well, during the county season, I'm going to play T20 cricket. I got my Hundred — can I get a few other franchise leagues?" And there's a lot of cricketers in that bracket. That bracket has a lot of money. But the slightly top tier where you're very skillful as a spin bowler — because you got good stock delivery — that probably depends on the boards: if they increase central contracts or even match fees for players.

队长沟通、年轻测试球员，与年轻一代的接入路径

Host: When you were playing in England, how did your captains kind of speak to you about it? What were the conversations? Did they differ from what they wanted from you?

Monty: The key was they wanted control from one end. They thought, "Well, if you go at two-and-a-halves, and you bowl with a good stock delivery to keep them in check, then in between that you bowl a bit wide of the crease, you go and take a bit of time out, go and speak to the fielder and say, 'What we going to have for lunch? What we going to have for dinner tonight?' The batsman's probably thinking, 'What's the new game plan?' And you just change your field, bowl the same delivery."

That kind of stuff is what sometimes, as a young cricketer, we don't talk about enough. Because if you're a young cricketer, you don't see a young 15-year-old or 16-year-old making test debut, but you see them in T20 formats. So you only got to follow what you see. And if you're a young cricketer, you're going to think to yourself, "I want to be a T20 cricketer."

So what could be great for test cricket is — is there a young cricketer? There's a young cricketer, A. Morgan, off Sema — he's only 18. Took a hat-trick. If he can be accelerated and play in the test team — we had Rehan Ahmed just after under-19s, he quickly played for England — more of those stories start happening, then they'll think, "Actually it's possible to play test cricket at a younger age," then T20 cricket.

Host: Yeah. Do we still have a pathways problem — and I say still because we definitely used to. Do we still have a pathways problem in cricket and making it accessible for enough people, enough kids, in this country?

Monty: Well, I think the ECB has recognized that. They're thinking maybe at state school we're not getting enough state school kids — we're getting maybe more private school kids. That's why there's a state school cricket program. There's cricket drones. There's one in Luton, I think Preston, there's going to be one in the West Midlands area. They're opening other areas — one in Bradford as well.

The ECB are recognizing in areas where cricket facilities are not great, they're investing money, creating these programs. There is a slightly disharmony — like they say, if you go to a private school, your coaching is going to be better, your cricket sense is going to be better. When you understand, you're going to speak to a cricket coach, they'll say, "Okay, this is what I need to do." While a state school may not have access to that knowledge. They are trying to enhance that, but there is that gap.

板球的语言、女子板球与全球受众扩张

Host: It's interesting — on the media front, found this as well. A lot of people came up and said it to me: "Oh, I don't understand a lot of the language that people use on cricket. Even if it's on the BBC feed." My wife, who loves cricket — but only really because, you know, as a girl born in the '90s, you didn't get the opportunity to play much — she goes onto BBC Sport and they'll be using all of the cricket language. If you've never played the game, if you've never had the exposure growing up — yeah, we get it, obviously I've been a part of it. But there was just a way of even communicating the game that needs to change. I know that's started to improve. From the media side when you've worked, especially on something like the Hundred — which is now built for this next generation — do you see, even at that level, things starting to shift and trying to make it more accessible, inclusive, understandable for people that haven't had the luxury of growing up in and around cricket environments?

Monty: That's a really interesting point. Coming from a British South Asian background, cricket's massive in India, in Pakistan. Even in England, you follow it, you understand the language. But if you're a neutral fan — let's say a big football fan, or rugby — but you're trying to go into cricket, I think the T20 and the Hundred has helped. We've seen ticket sales rise massively between the age of 25 — we see more people from all different backgrounds getting involved in cricket.

It's helped the female sport massively. The Hundred has been absolutely brilliant for the female cricketers. It's really accelerated their growth. We've got the T20 World Cup happening in England right now. You see the talent — every couple of years, the game is getting faster with the women's game as well. Which is great to see, because sport has always gone quicker and quicker. It's brilliant how the bats — they're getting faster, they're hitting the ball further, fielders are getting quicker. The women's game is accelerating at a really good rate now.

退役后的舞台：媒体、教练、club cricket 与"那一下击中柱头的感觉"

Host: If I said to you, post-cricket now — post-playing — mostly where you enjoy most, where you feel most valuable, where you have the most to add: where is that? Media? Coaching?

Monty: Look, the thing is, in cricket when you're playing for a country, you take a wicket and that buzz is unbelievable. You're trying to replicate that. You're always trying to find that same feeling. That's why sometimes I play a bit of club cricket — if I hit the top of off, even one bail throughout the whole club cricket summer, it makes my summer. That's it. That's all I need. It feels good.

And it's the same thing — what's the next thing that's going to give me the same feeling? That's why I did a course in sports journalism. So hopefully talking about the game from whatever perspective it is — it gives you a buzz. I love working as a commentator, as a pundit, working in the media — which gives me huge satisfaction. Coaching is another area which I would love to go into. It's lovely to help someone and then see them perform and do really well. You still feel connected in their journey. So both areas — I love doing.

"Seven Forms of Income"：第二段事业与组合式人生

Host: You mentioned as well there's a portfolio element to life after sport for a lot of athletes. What you mean by that is it's lots of different things that come together to make this new job — the second innings, as you perfectly described it earlier. Outside of that direct work with cricket, what have you been doing — business front, charitable front, etc.? How's that all packaged up for you?

Monty: So, you need — I was reading a book on finance. Self-help books, we all do, right? Make us all better again. So —

Host: What's the best one?

Monty: Well, I'm still trying to figure that one out.

Host: What's the worst one?

Monty: Oh, yeah. I don't want that to be bottom of the shelf of WH Smith. Why? If I mention that book —

Host: That's the power of the show.

Monty: Exactly. This is the power of this show. As you're going through — Simon Sinek, yeah, exactly — when you're going through Heathrow Airport and it's WH Smith, and I mentioned that book, and they think Monty mentioned it, and it's 50p, it's because of him.

Host: Yeah, exactly. I've dropped it down to the bottom of that shelf.

Monty: So I don't want to mention that book. There's many great books out there to help. But for me — I found that you need seven forms of income if you want to become a millionaire. Let's say I've got a goal — I want to become a millionaire. You need seven forms of income. That comes in: media, playing, coaching, after-dinner speaking, I do TV work, charitable work — which obviously I don't get paid for, but charitable work is great for networking, enhances business opportunities — and creating your own business opportunities. So there you go, I've got seven forms of income already.

That is the challenge for the retired sportsman: you continue to keep yourself busy, but actually having a business — have one or two businesses that is your major cash cow. That is always going to be the challenge for athletes: to develop that.

Host: Did anyone tell you that, or was that something you found out for yourself?

Monty: That's something I found out for myself. The challenge for me in the UK is: is there a business I can develop? Is there someone I can work with on a business that can actually be my longer-term project?

Host: How's it been leveraging the network that you built while playing to help you after your career?

Monty: It's been good. People are very helpful. People want to talk about cricket. I'm a big football fan. I love my boxing. I love all my sports. I love England doing well in all the sports. That's my biggest thing. Now I want England to win the World Cup. That's it. On this show, I'm telling you right now — England are going to win the World Cup.

Host: Do you think?

Monty: Yes, honestly, they're 100% going to win it. They've got all the access, all the resources. There's loads of Arsenal players in that England team right now, and that's going to be the reason they'll win it.

Host: You're going to tell me you're an Arsenal fan now.

Monty: Absolutely. I was there at the parade after 22 years — brought everyone together. I was loving every moment. It was the best feeling in the world.

Host: You're not a Luton fan?

Monty: I am a Luton fan as well.

Host: So you're both?

Monty: Yeah. I had that half-shirt, half Luton, half Arsenal. My loyalties were both. As a football fan, we have irrational behaviors. We have a local team and a Premiership team — a lot of cricket fans or football fans I know disagree with me. They go, "No, you got to have one team." But I speak to them — you have a local team, which is Luton, which I'm even still angry about. On the 93rd minute, they scored, and Steven scored in another game, and they got into the playoffs and Luton didn't. They missed out. But they did well — they won the cup this year against Stockport, which was brilliant.

Host: It must have been perfect for you having Jack Wilshere as manager — an Arsenal legend playing — sorry, managing your other team. That's almost the perfect marriage.

Monty: It is the perfect marriage for me. I wish all the best for Jack Wilshere. I want him to continue to make Luton Town a great club, because he's an Arsenal — played for Arsenal, one of the great Arsenal players. Also, he's going to help my Luton to get back in the Premiership.

Host: Having these different elements to you, these different layers — yes, you're a multiple-format cricketer, but there's also so much else around it that you can now give time to and build on and look to create value in. To some people that is quite scary, but it's also quite exciting, I guess — if you embrace it fully and you go, "What does this now look like for me?"

Monty: Exactly. Like I said, the challenge is: can I make my second innings equally successful as my first, or even beyond that? You're looking for a business opportunity that gives me the same buzz as turning a ball when I played for England. Media does it as well. When I do after-dinner speaking, I drop a few jokes — I love doing that, that's quite good fun. Playing gives me that energy. Media — talking about it — does as well. But the next challenge is: is there a business for me where it gives me the same opportunity, the same buzz, where I can actually take it to the next level?

"粉丝最想问什么" 与 "你最想被问什么"

Host: What do people ask you the most? Is there one thing — you say you're doing an after-dinner speech, or you're doing charity work, or you're doing some media — is there one thing that everyone comes back to? Is it the Ashes?

Monty: Yeah, they talk about my favourite wicket. They talk about my favourite moment in cricket. They'll talk about, "What is it like playing for England?" — all of these kind of things. It is a lot of fun, just to talk about my memories.

Host: Is there anything you'd like to be asked that you're not asked?

Monty: That's a great question. If there's one question I'd like the cricket fans to ask me, is: what did they enjoy about me as a cricketer, and what is it that they liked? That's something for you to ask. But for them to come to me and just say to me — a lot of people say to me, "You gave us a lot of enjoyment, the way you played your cricket, and the way you played it" — which was amazing. But I think that'd be one question.

Host: Can I answer it? Because I'm a fan, right? Look, there you go, I'll give you that answer. The way you played the game is the easy answer. But what it is is: it humanized it. Quite often elite athletes seem very, very far away from what you can achieve, because they won't give you some of the emotion from it. You see a wall, and you see a way of being that is quite blocked off — and that's often the mindset that elite athletes have to get into.

But you did it with a smile. If there was ever a moment to have a bit of fun, you had that fun. You would absolutely dial in when you needed to. But I personally, as a fan, remember watching you, and you know, at that time, cricket — enjoying it, like actually enjoying that interaction. And it would be noticeably different to how I'd feel watching other players. You bridged a lot of the very, very serious landscape of professional sport and made it accessible. You humanized, I think, playing professional cricket.

Monty: I think that's a really good point. Sports people are seen as superheroes, or some people that are distant away — not saying that you're not, of course. I don't know what superpowers I have, but I'm yet to discover. Yeah — big hands.

You look at someone like Lewis Hamilton, Anthony Joshua — you'll think they're so far away. Cristiano Ronaldo — these are like superhuman beings. When I played cricket — I'm not saying I'm on the same bracket as them — but I think the cricket fans felt more of a connection. They thought, "Oh, here's a club cricketer who's playing for England — we can connect with this guy."

Host: And enjoying it.

Monty: And enjoying it.

Host: They weren't afraid to enjoy it in the moment, which most people build their whole careers to get to. You played 50 times for England. It's not like, "Oh, you know, I dropped him for a couple of tests." Half a century of test matches over seven, eight years.

Monty: I wish I played 75. I would have got my MCC membership automatically.

Host: Is that what it is, 75?

Monty: So I missed out on 25. I'm not even on the associate list. I'm going to try and get that however I can.

品牌、赞助商，与数字时代的"无过滤"声音

Monty: It's interesting you talk about what I brought to cricket. I recently had a new bat sponsor up north — Farfire Crest, the bird — and I go to him, "How comes you look to sponsor me?" And he goes, "The reason I want you is because of visibility, because of your brand, your visibility. When we say the name Monty Panesar, it gives us enjoyment, because you bring joy to cricket. Everyone knows who you are, and a lot of people would see that — you'll get eyeballs on my cricket brand. I'd rather give my sponsorship to a cricketer who can bring eyeballs, and is obviously been very lucky to be popular when I played cricket, than someone who's highly skillful but doesn't have the brand or is not a popular cricketer."

That's really interesting — when I had that conversation, it gave me the incentive that actually I need to dial into this and make it my strength. When I'm working on any opportunities — like the media, like the coaching, like businesses — bring this is what I can bring to you guys. Then hopefully people can see the benefits and the value of it.

Host: Well, look — your YouTube channel that you've started is going to be a good way to do that. Because the best thing about those kind of things is that you are your own producer. You can create whatever content you want. No one's telling you how to be or what to do.

Monty: Absolutely. Sports people now — we live in the digital age. Digital journalism is massive at the moment. People want unfiltered, highly opinionated views on things that are happening in sport. The normal cricket fan — the fans are the biggest stakeholders in sport — they want to hear that. They don't want to hear just the same old boring stuff. They want to hear proper in-your-face kind of stuff, highly opinionated, highly judged views — because that's what creates the interest and the traction.

收尾

Host: Look, Monty, it's been amazing having you in to chat. As I said, from watching you for so many years as a fan, you get an impression — and I'm so pleased to see that that impression actually is genuine and real. It's been a lot of fun having a chat with you, and I think you've got some really exciting stuff going forward. But thank you so much for showing an interest, and thank you for wanting to come and join us — because it is a really, really fun thing to do when we get a person that's engaged as you have been today.

Monty: No, thank you so much for the opportunity, for giving me the platform to talk about my journey, about correlation between sports and business, and also my cricketing journey. And hopefully — England win the World Cup.

Host: England win the World Cup.

Monty: And I said it here first, on your podcast.

Host: Said it here first. It'll make a great clip. Thank you.

Monty: Yeah, thank you.

观看原视频 →
item.description:
## 一、最反常识的观察：Monty 的"大手"是一个被忽略的隐喻系统
整段访谈中最容易被一笑而过、却最值得停下来思考的细节，是他反复提到自己的手——比 Tyson Fury 还大的手。表面上这是闲聊，但你把它和他的整段叙事并置看，会发现一种**身体资本（physical capital）向品牌资本（brand capital）的隐秘转译**：
- 年轻时，"大手"是他能从左臂快投转型左臂旋的生物力学前提（biomechanics → 技术）； - 中年时，"大手"成了播客里被反复消费的视觉梗（biomechanics → meme）； - 现在，球拍赞助商 Farfire Crest 看中的不是他的技术指标，而是"你的名字一被说出来，人们就会笑"（meme → eyeballs → money）。
也就是说，他的"大手"经历了**三次资产证券化**：从运动器官，变成技术差异点，再变成可被赞助商定价的注意力资产。运动员退役后的真正难题，不是"再就业"，而是**找到那个能在三种资本之间反复转换的底层资产**。Monty 自己未必完全意识到这一点，但他在用这双手讲一个关于资产形态变化
